A $7M wrongful death settlement (2024) and multiple ACLU suits document serious conditions problems at LaSalle-operated ICE facilities. Tagged workers_ice_cooperation.

firms.name: Nissin Foods Holdings Co., Ltd.
firms.summary: Nissin Foods Holdings (TYO: 2897), headquartered in Osaka, Japan, is the inventor of instant noodles and the world’s largest instant noodle producer. Founded by Momofuku Ando in 1948, the company launched Cup Noodles in 1971 and Top Ramen in the United States, together commanding an estimated 30–40% of global instant noodle sales. Every major Nissin instant noodle product is classified as NOVA Group 4 (ultra-processed) under the NOVA food classification system, which categorizes foods by degree of industrial processing rather than nutrient content (Monteiro et al., 2019, NOVA classification system, Public Health Nutrition, PMC6322572). A 2014 study from the Harvard School of Public Health (Oh et al., Journal of Nutrition) found that women who consumed instant noodles two or more times per week had a 68% higher risk of metabolic syndrome — a cluster of conditions including hypertension, insulin resistance, and abdominal obesity — independent of overall dietary quality. Cup Noodles (original flavor) contains 1,190 mg sodium per serving (52% of the daily recommended value), TBHQ (a preservative rated “Avoid” by CSPI’s Chemical Cuisine), MSG, sodium phosphates, and multiple artificial flavor compounds.

firms.name: Smithfield Foods (WH Group)
firms.summary: The world's largest pork processor and hog producer, headquartered in Smithfield, Virginia. Acquired by Chinese conglomerate WH Group (formerly Shuanghui International, HKEX: 288) in September 2013 for $7.1 billion — the largest Chinese acquisition of a US company at the time. Smithfield's processed pork portfolio — Eckrich smoked sausages, Armour Star canned meats, Farmland bacon, Gwaltney, John Morrell, and Nathan's Famous hot dogs — relies on sodium nitrite as a preservative and color fixative. CSPI's Chemical Cuisine rates sodium nitrite as 'Avoid' because it reacts with amines in stomach acid to form N-nitrosamines, potent carcinogens. In 2015, IARC classified consumption of processed meats — all of which require sodium nitrite for their characteristic cured color and flavor — as Group 1 carcinogenic to humans, the same tier as tobacco smoke. Smithfield generates over $15 billion in annual revenue and is one of the largest single distributors of sodium nitrite-preserved meats to American consumers.

firms.name: Costco Wholesale
firms.summary: Costco Wholesale is the second-largest retailer in the US, known for industry-leading employee compensation. In 2023, Costco raised its minimum starting wage to $19.50/hour, well above federal minimums, and has long offered health insurance to part-time workers who log at least 24 hours per week. Turnover rates consistently run far below retail industry averages, and its profit-sharing and stock-purchase programs have made many long-tenured employees wealthy.

firms.name: Patagonia Inc.
firms.summary: Patagonia is a private outdoor apparel company founded by Yvon Chouinard in 1973. In 2022, Chouinard transferred ownership to the Patagonia Purpose Trust and the Holdfast Collective, a climate-focused nonprofit, ensuring all future profits fight climate change rather than flow to private enrichment. Patagonia pays all employees a living wage, maintains on-site childcare at its California headquarters, and is a founding member of 1% for the Planet.

firms.name: REI Co-op
firms.summary: REI (Recreational Equipment, Inc.) is the largest consumer cooperative in the United States, with over 22 million lifetime members and more than 15,000 employees. As a cooperative, all profits are returned to members as dividends or reinvested in the business. Employees earn above-market wages with profit-sharing, generous PTO, and subsidized outdoor adventures. REI has appeared on Fortune's '100 Best Companies to Work For' list multiple times.

firms.name: Dr. Bronner's
firms.summary: Dr. Bronner's is a family-owned soap company based in Vista, California. The company caps executive pay at 5x the lowest-paid full-time employee's salary and pays a minimum of $24/hour to all US employees as of 2023. It funds employee profit-sharing, on-site childcare assistance, and covers 100% of healthcare premiums. Dr. Bronner's donates approximately 50% of profits to causes including drug policy reform, regenerative agriculture, and fair trade.

firms.name: Gravity Payments
firms.summary: Gravity Payments is a credit card processing company based in Seattle, Washington. In 2015, CEO Dan Price announced he would cut his own salary from $1.1 million to $70,000 per year and use those funds to raise the minimum salary for all employees to $70,000. The decision became a widely studied case in income equality and its effects on worker productivity and retention. By 2023, the company minimum had risen to $80,000.

firms.name: WinCo Foods
firms.summary: WinCo Foods is a privately held, employee-owned supermarket chain operating primarily in the western United States. Employees can participate in the company's pension/profit-sharing plan, which has accumulated over $650 million in retirement assets for workers. WinCo grocery clerks consistently earn above-average wages for the industry, and the company has avoided mass layoffs through its employee-ownership structure.

firms.name: Publix Super Markets
firms.summary: Publix Super Markets is the largest employee-owned supermarket chain in the United States. Employees own approximately 80% of the company's stock through profit-sharing and stock-purchase plans available to all workers after one year of service. Publix has appeared on Fortune's '100 Best Companies to Work For' list for over 25 consecutive years, and employee ownership has created thousands of ordinary grocery workers with substantial retirement wealth.

firms.name: King Arthur Baking Company
firms.summary: King Arthur Baking Company (formerly King Arthur Flour) is a 100% employee-owned company headquartered in Norwich, Vermont. The founders sold the company to employees through an ESOP in 2004. The company is also a certified B Corporation, maintaining high standards for worker welfare, community impact, and environmental stewardship. Employee-owners benefit from profit-sharing, flexible scheduling, and a collaborative governance model.

firms.name: Eileen Fisher Inc.
firms.summary: Eileen Fisher Inc. is a sustainable women's clothing brand that has transferred over 30% of company ownership to employees through its EILEEN FISHER Leadership Awards program. The company operates a take-back program (Renew) for used garments, pays living wages throughout its supply chain, and invests in women's leadership initiatives globally. Eileen Fisher employees earn competitive wages and participate in a profit-sharing program.

firms.name: Danone North America
firms.summary: Danone North America is a subsidiary of the French food company Danone SA that became the world's largest Certified B Corporation in 2018. The company employs over 6,000 people across the US and Canada and has committed to paying all employees a living wage as defined by MIT's Living Wage Calculator. Danone North America produces brands including Stonyfield Organic, Silk, So Delicious, and Vega.

firms.name: Allbirds
firms.summary: Allbirds is a public footwear and apparel company (Nasdaq: BIRD) that uses sustainable materials including merino wool, eucalyptus tree fiber, and sugarcane. It became a certified B Corporation in 2016 and prints a carbon footprint label on every product. Allbirds targets carbon-neutral operations and was the first footwear company to use the Higg Materials Sustainability Index to disclose the environmental impact of every product.

firms.name: Ecosia GmbH
firms.summary: Ecosia is a German social enterprise that operates a search engine and donates approximately 80% of its advertising revenue to tree-planting organizations worldwide. As of 2024, Ecosia has financed the planting of over 200 million trees in more than 35 countries. The company's articles of association prevent shareholders from extracting profits beyond a reasonable return, legally locking in the tree-planting mission.

firms.name: Who Gives A Crap
firms.summary: Who Gives A Crap is an Australian social enterprise that makes recycled and bamboo toilet paper, donating 50% of its profits to build toilets and improve sanitation in the developing world. All products are made without plastic packaging, from 100% recycled or FSC-certified bamboo. The company has donated over $13 million to sanitation causes since founding in 2013 and is a certified B Corporation.

firms.name: Tentree International
firms.summary: Tentree International is a Canadian apparel brand that plants ten trees for every item purchased. The company is a certified B Corporation and aims to plant one billion trees by 2030. Products are made from sustainable materials including organic cotton, Tencel, and recycled polyester. Tentree partners with Eden Reforestation Projects and other certified organizations to verify tree-planting impact.

firms.name: Pela Case Inc.
firms.summary: Pela Case is a Canadian company making compostable phone cases and accessories from Flaxstic, a proprietary bioplastic made from flax straw waste. The company is a certified B Corporation and is committed to being plastic-free by 2028. Pela collects and composts returned cases through its Pela 360 take-back program, creating a closed-loop system for phone accessories.

firms.name: Grove Collaborative
firms.summary: Grove Collaborative is a public company (NYSE: GROV) and certified B Corporation that sells natural home and personal care products. In 2021, it became the first plastic-neutral retailer in the US, offsetting all plastic in its product catalog. Grove has committed to being 100% plastic-free by 2025 and has a rigorous ingredient standard that screens out over 130 harmful chemicals from products it sells.

firms.name: Blueland Inc.
firms.summary: Blueland is a New York-based cleaning company that makes tablet-based cleaning products designed to eliminate single-use plastic bottles. Customers purchase a reusable bottle once and refill it with dissolvable tablets, dramatically reducing plastic waste. The company is a certified B Corporation and has prevented over 1 billion single-use plastic bottles from being produced since launching in 2019.

firms.name: Cotopaxi LLC
firms.summary: Cotopaxi is a certified B Corporation and outdoor gear brand dedicated to alleviating global poverty and protecting the environment. The company donates at least 1% of annual revenue to its Cotopaxi Foundation, which funds poverty alleviation programs. Cotopaxi uses recycled materials in product lines such as its Del Dia bags, made from factory remnant fabrics, and is a founding member of 1% for the Planet.

firms.name: Numi Organic Tea
firms.summary: Numi Organic Tea is a certified B Corporation and family-owned tea company based in Oakland, California. All teas are certified USDA Organic and Fair Trade, and packaging uses minimal plastic with compostable tea bags free of plastic mesh. The company is carbon-neutral and operates under a rigorous environmental and social impact framework that includes supporting tea farmers through fair trade premiums.

firms.name: Preserve Products (Recycline)
firms.summary: Preserve Products (founded as Recycline) is a Massachusetts company that makes personal care and kitchen products from 100% recycled plastic, including #5 polypropylene that most curbside programs do not accept. Products include toothbrushes, razors, tableware, and food storage containers. The company pioneered closed-loop manufacturing for consumer products and partners with Stonyfield Organic to recycle yogurt cups into toothbrushes.

firms.name: Lush Cosmetics Ltd.
firms.summary: Lush Cosmetics is a British private company owned by the Weir family that manufactures and sells handmade cosmetics without animal testing. Lush has been against animal testing since its founding in 1995 and campaigns actively for a global ban. The company is Leaping Bunny certified and does not sell products in markets that require animal testing by law, which led to its exit from mainland China.

firms.name: e.l.f. Beauty Inc.
firms.summary: e.l.f. Beauty (NYSE: ELF) is an American cosmetics company that has been 100% vegan and cruelty-free since its founding in 2004. The company holds Leaping Bunny certification from Cruelty Free International, which requires that no animal testing occurs at any stage of product development, including by ingredient suppliers. e.l.f. was one of the first major cosmetics companies to achieve and maintain 100% vegan status across its entire product line.

firms.name: Pacifica Beauty Inc.
firms.summary: Pacifica Beauty is a privately held beauty company based in Portland, Oregon, that makes 100% vegan and cruelty-free cosmetics, skincare, and fragrance products. All products are certified vegan by Vegan Action and cruelty-free under Leaping Bunny standards. Pacifica also formulates without parabens, phthalates, and sulfates, making it a dual leader in both animal welfare and clean beauty.

firms.name: Derma E Inc.
firms.summary: Derma E is a California-based skincare company that has been cruelty-free and vegan since its founding in 1984. Products are certified vegan, Leaping Bunny certified, and formulated without parabens, sodium lauryl sulfates, petrolatum, artificial colors, and synthetic fragrances. Derma E is PETA-certified cruelty-free and one of the longest-standing vegan skincare brands in the US market.

firms.name: 100% PURE Inc.
firms.summary: 100% PURE is a California-based beauty brand that makes products pigmented with fruit, vegetable, and tea extracts rather than synthetic dyes, and formulated without any animal-derived ingredients. All products are certified vegan and cruelty-free, and the company uses minimal packaging. Founded in 2004 with a mission to create the healthiest and most pure beauty products possible.

firms.name: Meow Meow Tweet
firms.summary: Meow Meow Tweet is a small-batch, vegan personal care brand founded in Brooklyn in 2009. All products are 100% vegan with no animal-derived ingredients, cruelty-free, and packaged in minimal plastic-free materials. The company makes deodorants, lotions, lip balms, and face care products using simple botanical ingredients. Meow Meow Tweet is certified vegan by Vegan Action.

firms.name: Herbivore Botanicals LLC
firms.summary: Herbivore Botanicals is a Seattle-based skincare company founded in 2011 that makes 100% vegan and cruelty-free skincare products. All formulas use natural plant and mineral ingredients without synthetic preservatives. Products are certified cruelty-free and have never contained animal-derived ingredients. The company uses minimalist recyclable packaging and manufactures in small batches in the US.

firms.name: W.S. Badger Company
firms.summary: W.S. Badger Company is a family-owned certified B Corporation based in Gilsum, New Hampshire, making organic personal care products. The company is Leaping Bunny certified and clearly labels all vegan products. Badger is certified USDA Organic and donates 10% of after-tax profits to charitable causes. The family-owned structure ensures that ethical practices cannot be abandoned for short-term profit.

firms.name: Cocokind Inc.
firms.summary: Cocokind is a San Francisco-based certified B Corporation that makes affordable clean skincare products. All products are 100% vegan and cruelty-free, certified by PETA's Beauty Without Bunnies program. Cocokind is notable for publishing a 'transparency scorecard' on each product showing its carbon footprint, packaging weight, and supply chain distance — a level of disclosure rare in the beauty industry.

firms.name: Ursa Major Inc.
firms.summary: Ursa Major is a Vermont-based clean skincare brand founded in 2010 that makes 100% vegan and cruelty-free products. The company bans over 1,400 questionable ingredients from its formulas and is certified cruelty-free by Leaping Bunny. Products are made in the US without synthetic fragrances, parabens, sulfates, or any animal-derived ingredients.

firms.name: Nature's Path Foods
firms.summary: Nature's Path Foods is a family-owned organic food company based in Richmond, British Columbia, and one of the largest independent certified organic food companies in North America. All products are USDA Certified Organic, and the company does not use GMOs, artificial colors, flavors, or preservatives. Nature's Path has been certified organic since 1985 and donates 1% of net sales to environmental charities.

firms.name: Bob's Red Mill Natural Foods
firms.summary: Bob's Red Mill is an employee-owned natural foods company based in Milwaukie, Oregon, that specializes in whole grain and minimally processed flour, oats, grains, and baking ingredients. Founder Bob Moore transferred full ownership to employees through an ESOP in 2020. Products are stone-milled using traditional methods and many are certified gluten-free, organic, and non-GMO. The company refuses to use artificial additives or preservatives.

firms.name: Siggi's Dairy
firms.summary: Siggi's is an Icelandic-style skyr brand founded in New York City in 2004. Products are made with simple ingredients — milk, live cultures, and fruit — with no artificial sweeteners, preservatives, or added colors. Siggi's yogurts consistently rank among the lowest in added sugar of any flavored yogurt and use only non-GMO ingredients.

firms.name: Once Upon a Farm Inc.
firms.summary: Once Upon a Farm is a certified B Corporation that makes cold-pressed organic baby food, kids' snacks, and smoothies. Founded in 2015, the company uses USDA Organic ingredients with no added sugars, preservatives, or artificial ingredients. Products are cold-pressed to preserve nutrients rather than heat-pasteurized, a key distinction from conventional baby food brands.

firms.name: Hu Kitchen LLC
firms.summary: Hu Kitchen is a New York-based food brand known for making chocolate and snacks with radically simple, real-food ingredients. All products are made without refined sugar (using organic coconut sugar), without emulsifiers, without cane sugar refining agents, and without any additives. Hu's founding principle is to make food with ingredients a human can pronounce. All products are paleo-friendly, gluten-free, and certified USDA Organic.

firms.name: Kite Hill LLC
firms.summary: Kite Hill is a California-based plant-based dairy company that makes almond milk-based yogurts, cream cheeses, ricotta, and other dairy alternatives using clean, minimal ingredients without gums, starches, or stabilizers that are common in plant-based dairy. Products are Non-GMO Project Verified and made with simple fermentation processes.

firms.name: Good Culture Inc.
firms.summary: Good Culture is a California-based dairy brand focused on clean cottage cheese and sour cream made with just milk and live cultures — no gum additives, no preservatives, and no artificial ingredients. The company uses milk from pasture-raised cows and USDA Organic ingredients. Good Culture is credited with reviving the cottage cheese category by proving clean-label dairy products can compete with additive-laden alternatives.

firms.name: Purely Elizabeth LLC
firms.summary: Purely Elizabeth is a Colorado-based certified B Corporation that makes organic granola, oatmeal, and snacks with clean, whole-food ingredients. All products are USDA Organic, gluten-free certified, and Non-GMO Project Verified. The company uses ancient grains, coconut sugar instead of refined sugar, and coconut oil, without any artificial additives. Purely Elizabeth donates 1% of sales to environmental causes through 1% for the Planet membership.

firms.name: Harmless Harvest Inc.
firms.summary: Harmless Harvest is a certified B Corporation that produces raw coconut water using a cold-pressure process (high-pressure processing) that preserves natural nutrients, enzymes, and flavor without heat pasteurization. The company sources fair-trade organic coconuts from Thailand and the product is USDA Organic certified. Harmless Harvest coconut water is free from additives, sweeteners, and preservatives, with no 'natural flavors' added.

firms.name: Miyoko's Creamery Inc.
firms.summary: Miyoko's Creamery is a California-based certified B Corporation that makes plant-based butter, cream cheese, and artisan cheeses from cashew and oat milk with minimal, recognizable ingredients. Founded by cookbook author Miyoko Schinner in 2014, the company uses traditional cheesemaking techniques adapted for plant-based dairy. Products are USDA Organic, Non-GMO Project Verified, and formulated without carrageenan, artificial ingredients, or excessive starches.

firms.name: Organic Valley (CROPP Cooperative)
firms.summary: Organic Valley is a farmer-owned cooperative (CROPP Cooperative) based in LaFarge, Wisconsin, with over 1,600 farmer-members. All farm profits return to farmer-owners through democratic cooperative governance. Founded in 1988, it is one of the largest organic food brands in the US. Because it is farmer-owned rather than investor-owned, it cannot be acquired by private equity and has protected farmer livelihoods for over 35 years.

firms.name: Ocean Spray Cranberries Inc.
firms.summary: Ocean Spray is an agricultural cooperative owned by approximately 700 grower-members across the US, Canada, and Chile. Founded in 1930, the cooperative returns all profits to its farmer-owners through a patronage dividend model. Farmers participate in governance through democratic elections, and the cooperative structure prevents private equity acquisition or short-term profit extraction at farmer expense.

firms.name: Land O'Lakes Inc.
firms.summary: Land O'Lakes is a member-owned agricultural cooperative headquartered in Arden Hills, Minnesota, with nearly 1,700 member cooperatives and approximately 300,000 farmer-owners. Founded in 1921, it is one of the largest farmer-owned cooperatives in the US with approximately $15 billion in annual revenue. The cooperative structure ensures profits flow back to farmer-members rather than outside investors.

firms.name: Tillamook County Creamery Association
firms.summary: Tillamook County Creamery Association is an Oregon-based farmer-owned cooperative that has been making cheese and dairy products since 1909. The co-op is owned by approximately 70 farm families in Tillamook County who collectively share profits and govern the organization democratically. Tillamook generated over $1 billion in revenue in 2021, all of which flows back to its farm-owner members rather than outside shareholders.

firms.name: Cabot Creamery Cooperative
firms.summary: Cabot Creamery is a farmer-owned dairy cooperative based in Vermont, founded in 1919 and owned by Agri-Mark, a cooperative of approximately 800 family farms in New England and New York. Cabot is a certified B Corporation — one of the first cooperatives to achieve B Corp status — and returns all profits to its farmer-members. The cooperative structure provides dairy farmers with market stability unavailable to those selling on commodity markets.

firms.name: USAA (United Services Automobile Association)
firms.summary: USAA is a Texas-based diversified financial services company that serves US military members, veterans, and their families. It is structured as a reciprocal exchange — a member-owned organization — not a stock-owned corporation. Members are both customers and owners, and profits are distributed back to members in the form of dividends and reduced rates. USAA consistently rates at the top of consumer satisfaction surveys in banking and insurance.

firms.name: The Vanguard Group Inc.
firms.summary: Vanguard is an American investment management company structured as a mutual company — it is owned by the funds it manages, which are in turn owned by investors. This means Vanguard has no outside shareholders and all economies of scale are passed directly to investors as lower fees. Founded by John Bogle in 1975 specifically to create a fund company that could not extract profits for private shareholders, Vanguard manages approximately $8 trillion in assets.

firms.name: Ace Hardware Corporation
firms.summary: Ace Hardware is a retailer-owned cooperative, meaning all Ace Hardware stores are independently owned and the cooperative itself is owned by its retail members rather than outside investors. Founded in 1924, the cooperative has approximately 5,700 stores in over 70 countries. Ace Hardware consistently ranks highest in customer satisfaction in the home improvement category. The cooperative model allows independent local hardware stores to compete with large chains without ceding ownership to outside investors.

firms.name: Equal Exchange Inc.
firms.summary: Equal Exchange is a 100% worker-owned cooperative based in West Bridgewater, Massachusetts, that imports and distributes fair trade coffee, tea, chocolate, and snacks. Founded in 1986, it operates under full worker ownership — all employees are worker-owners who participate in democratic governance. Equal Exchange partners directly with small farmer cooperatives, bypassing commodity traders and paying prices that support farmer communities.

firms.name: Wakefern Food Corporation
firms.summary: Wakefern Food Corporation is the largest retailer-owned cooperative in the United States and the parent cooperative of the ShopRite supermarket chain. Founded in 1946, Wakefern is owned by approximately 50 independent retail members who operate over 350 ShopRite stores across the northeastern US. The cooperative structure allows independent grocery store owners to pool resources and compete with corporate chains without ceding ownership to outside investors.

firms.name: Basecamp LLC (37signals)
firms.summary: 37signals (operating as Basecamp) is a Chicago-based software company founded by Jason Fried and David Heinemeier Hansson that makes project management software (Basecamp) and email (HEY). The company has taken no outside investment, has no board of directors to answer to, and operates with full financial transparency. The founders publish annual profit figures and actively advocate for bootstrapping and building a profitable company without venture capital or private equity.

firms.name: Buffer Inc.
firms.summary: Buffer is a social media management company that has practiced radical salary transparency since 2013, publicly publishing every employee's salary, the formula used to calculate it, and the equity structure. The company also transparently shares company revenue and financial metrics publicly. Buffer is remote-first and has deliberately avoided pursuit of unicorn-status growth in favor of a sustainable business operating with full transparency.

firms.name: Kickstarter PBC
firms.summary: Kickstarter converted from a standard corporation to a Public Benefit Corporation (PBC) in 2021, legally enshrining obligations to artists, employees, and communities in its corporate charter. As a PBC, the company's charter prohibits it from prioritizing shareholder returns above its public benefit mission. Kickstarter's charter commits the company to never sell user data to advertisers, to donate 5% of after-tax profits to arts and music education, and to fight inequality.

firms.name: Mozilla Foundation
firms.summary: The Mozilla Foundation is a nonprofit organization that oversees the Mozilla open-source project, including the Firefox browser and Thunderbird email client. The foundation operates with full financial transparency, publishing annual reports with income and expenditures. Mozilla's mission is explicitly to keep the internet public and open rather than controlled by a small number of powerful corporations. The organization actively advocates for internet policy that limits corporate and government surveillance.

firms.name: Wikimedia Foundation Inc.
firms.summary: The Wikimedia Foundation is a San Francisco-based nonprofit that operates Wikipedia and related open knowledge projects. It operates with full financial transparency, publishing audited annual reports and detailed breakdowns of all expenditures. The foundation accepts no advertising and all Wikimedia content is freely licensed for anyone to use, embodying the principle that knowledge should not be controlled by private interests.

firms.name: DuckDuckGo Inc.
firms.summary: DuckDuckGo is a privately held internet privacy company that operates a search engine and privacy-focused browser. The company does not collect personal data, does not build user profiles, and does not share search data with third parties. Founded in 2008 by Gabriel Weinberg, DuckDuckGo's business model relies on contextual advertising rather than surveillance advertising, proving that a profitable search engine can operate without mass data collection.

firms.name: Proton AG
firms.summary: Proton AG is a Swiss company founded by CERN scientists in 2014 that makes end-to-end encrypted email (ProtonMail), calendar, VPN, and cloud storage. The company is majority-owned by the Proton Foundation, a Swiss nonprofit, which prevents it from being sold to surveillance-oriented corporate interests. Proton operates under strict Swiss privacy law and cannot be compelled to share user data with foreign governments.

firms.name: Signal Foundation
firms.summary: Signal Foundation is a 501(c)(3) nonprofit that develops and operates the Signal encrypted messaging app. Signal collects minimal metadata and cannot read user messages. The foundation was created in 2018 by WhatsApp co-founder Brian Acton (who left after Facebook's acquisition) with a $50 million donation. The nonprofit structure ensures Signal cannot be sold or monetized through surveillance. The Signal Protocol underpins encryption in WhatsApp, Google Messages, and other apps.

firms.name: Fairphone B.V.
firms.summary: Fairphone is a Dutch social enterprise that manufactures smartphones with radical supply chain transparency, using conflict-free minerals and publishing full supplier lists. The company is structured as a social enterprise with a charter that prioritizes people and planet over profit. Fairphone publishes the full environmental cost of its phones and designs them for repairability with user-replaceable batteries and modules.

firms.name: Credo Beauty Inc.
firms.summary: Credo Beauty is a specialty clean beauty retailer that publishes the most detailed ingredient standards in the retail beauty industry. The 'Credo Clean Standard' bans over 2,700 ingredients and requires brands to disclose all ingredients including fragrance components — a level of transparency that major retailers refuse to demand. Credo holds its supplier brands to binding standards and publicly calls out greenwashing, using transparency as a direct check on industry power.

firms.name: Leonard Green & Partners
firms.summary: Los Angeles-based PE firm founded 1989 with $85B AUM. Drove Joann Fabrics to bankruptcy twice (2024, 2025) after saddling it with $1.1B debt following 2010 LBO, resulting in liquidation and 19,000 layoffs. Also extracted $658M in dividends from Prospect Medical Holdings (2010–2021), leaving the 16-hospital safety net system with $2.3B debt before January 2025 bankruptcy.

firms.name: Audax Private Equity
firms.summary: Boston-based PE firm with $19.5B AUM. Created Renovo home remodeling platform in 2021 by rolling up seven regional contractors, saddled it with $150M debt from BlackRock/Apollo/Oaktree, then exited in 2024. Company collapsed October 2025 with $100M+ liabilities and $50k assets, leaving 1,500+ employees without final paychecks and thousands of homeowners with unfinished projects.

firms.name: Saudi Arabia Public Investment Fund
firms.summary: Saudi Arabia's sovereign wealth fund. Acquired 93.4% of Electronic Arts in $55B LBO (2025), the largest leveraged buyout in history. Deal financed with $20B debt from JPMorgan, loading EA with significant leverage. PIF has investments across gaming, sports, and tech sectors.

firms.name: BlackRock
firms.summary: World's largest asset manager. Led $40B acquisition of Aligned Data Centers (2025) via AI Infrastructure Partnership consortium including Microsoft, Nvidia, MGX, and sovereign wealth funds. Deal structured with significant debt leverage targeting $100B total including debt financing.

firms.name: Platinum Equity
firms.summary: Beverly Hills PE firm founded 1995 by Tom Gores with $48B AUM and 60 portfolio companies employing 200,000 people. Drove LifeScan (glucose monitors) to July 2025 bankruptcy after loading it with $1.9B debt from its 2018 $2.1B LBO, eliminating 75% of $1.7B debt through Chapter 11. Also drove aerospace supplier Incora to bankruptcy June 2023 with $3.14B debt, and Elevate Textiles to debt-for-equity restructuring May 2023, reducing Platinum's stake to 2%.

firms.name: NRDC (National Realty & Development Corp.)
firms.summary: Real estate-focused PE firm led by Richard Baker, founded 2005 explicitly 'to snap up retailers with valuable real estate.' Systematically acquired Lord & Taylor, Hudson's Bay, Saks, Galeria Kaufhof, and Neiman Marcus—each eventually filed bankruptcy, though Baker often profited on real estate extraction. Saks Fifth Avenue filed Chapter 11 January 2026 with $3B debt after Baker's 2024 Neiman merger financed by $2.2B junk bonds. Baker admitted in 2017: 'Being a retailer, that is too tough' but real estate 'is a place where we can make a lot of money.'
firms.notableBk: Saks Fifth Avenue (2026), Lord & Taylor, Hudson's Bay

firms.name: Authentic Brands Group
firms.summary: Brand licensing PE-backed entity founded 2010 by Jamie Salter, controlling 50+ consumer brands generating $38B annual retail sales. Business model: acquire brand IP from bankruptcies, license to operators who bear operational risk. Bought Forever 21 from 2020 bankruptcy for $81M, split IP from operations; operating entity filed bankruptcy March 2025 with $1.5B debt, liquidating 355 stores and laying off 9,200 workers while ABG retained brand rights. Also acquired Brooks Brothers, Lucky Brand, Barneys (liquidated), and Aéropostale from bankruptcy. CEO Salter called Forever 21 his 'biggest mistake.'

firms.name: Hyatt Hotels Corporation
firms.summary: Global hotel chain with brands including Park Hyatt, Grand Hyatt, Andaz, and Hyatt Regency across 78 countries. Executive Chairman Thomas Pritzker maintained regular email contact with Jeffrey Epstein from at least 2010 through early 2019, exchanging 20+ back-and-forth emails even after Epstein's 2008 conviction for sex crimes involving minors. Pritzker was named in Virginia Giuffre's deposition and resigned from Hyatt's board in February 2026, stating he 'deeply regrets' his association with Epstein, as documented in unsealed DOJ files.

firms.name: U.S. News & World Report (Zuckerman ownership)
firms.summary: Publisher and media company owned by billionaire Mortimer Zuckerman, who also co-founded Boston Properties. Unsealed Epstein documents reveal Zuckerman communicated regularly with Jeffrey Epstein, arranging numerous dinners and meetings at Epstein's Manhattan home from at least 2008 through 2015. In 2008, Zuckerman sought Epstein's advice on estate planning, sharing his will and a $1.9 billion asset evaluation. Epstein leveraged this relationship to influence New York Daily News coverage of abuse allegations against him, with Zuckerman replying that the paper was 'doing major editing over huge objections' to accommodate Epstein's requests.

firms.name: Microsoft Corporation
firms.summary: Global technology corporation co-founded by Bill Gates, who remains on the board. Unsealed DOJ documents released in January 2026 show several hundred references to Gates, including emails about philanthropic projects, calendar entries documenting meetings, and photos from events both attended. Gates met with Epstein multiple times from 2011 to 2014, years after Epstein's 2008 conviction, continuing even after his then-wife Melinda French Gates raised concerns in 2013. The Gates Foundation commissioned an external investigation in April 2026 ahead of Bill Gates' congressional testimony about the relationship.

brands.why: Vista immediately laid off 15% (~2,250 jobs). Discussed raising prices 30–40%. Banks lost $1.3B financing the deal. Employees were locked out of accounts before receiving layoff notices.
Sold to PE after rejecting a $17B offer months earlier. Now private and focused on exit returns. Customers lose public company accountability. Price hikes expected.
Thoma Bravo laid off hundreds immediately and offshored roles. Glassdoor reviews describe 'cutting 80% of the company.' PE managing for EBITDA exit, not product quality.
Taken private from SAP. All PE exits require price hikes or cuts to generate returns. Customer data inside PE-owned platforms faces monetization pressure.
Thoma Bravo took SolarWinds private in 2016 then re-IPO'd it. During PE ownership, the 2020 cyberattack hit 18,000+ organizations. PE cost-cutting on security contributed.

Blackstone closed Jan 2025. Social media already documents portion reductions. Classic early-stage PE quality extraction — typically manifests in 2–3 years. Fast-food franchise menu is high in sodium and processed meats; a regular sub can contain over 1,500 mg sodium (65%+ of daily recommended intake).

Sold by Dobson family after 69 years. Texas Monthly 2025 documented quality concerns. PE expands rapidly on debt then cuts quality to service it. Menu is high in sodium, saturated fat, and processed ingredients; a Whataburger meal can exceed 1,200 calories and 2,000 mg sodium.
Acquired by Blackstone June 2024. Franchise fee pressure and cost-cutting typically follow within 2–3 years of PE acquisition.

Apollo portfolio company. PE-owned chains typically cut ingredient quality to improve EBITDA for exit. Chipotle remains founder-culture focused. High-calorie, high-sodium menu; a standard burrito can exceed 1,000 calories and 2,000 mg sodium before additions. Owned by Apollo Global, co-founded by Leon Black, who paid $158 million to Jeffrey Epstein in advisory fees over five years (documented in a 2021 independent review by Dechert LLP).

PE-engineered sale-leaseback stripped real estate and raised rents above market. 100 locations closed, 36,000 jobs affected in 2024. Chain restaurant preparations are typically high in sodium, butter, and deep-fried items; Cheddar Bay Biscuits alone contain 150 mg sodium each.

One of 10 PE-backed restaurant chains that went bankrupt in 2024. PE debt made adapting to post-COVID dining shifts impossible. Menu items routinely exceed 1,500 calories and 3,000 mg sodium per entrée; CSPI has repeatedly flagged TGI Fridays as one of the worst chain-restaurant offenders for portion size and sodium.
JAB's CEO admitted in 2025: smaller portions, higher prices, lower-quality ingredients. Switching to frozen bread — ending the fresh-baked identity. Two failed IPOs.

JAB acquired 2016, re-IPO'd 2021 but retains majority control. Rapid expansion to 14,100+ locations diluted the quality that built the brand. Ultra-processed, high-sugar product; a single Original Glazed doughnut contains 10g of sugar and 190 calories, and the brand actively markets dozen-box purchases as routine consumption.
JAB's first coffee monopoly acquisition. Once independent craft brand now part of a $120B extraction machine. Sourcing subordinated to margin targets.
JAB converted 88 Caribou locations to Peet's post-acquisition. Classic PE: eliminate redundancy, extract brand equity, standardize at lowest cost.
JAB paid $374M to take Einstein Noah private in 2014, then absorbed into Panera Brands 2021. Your dollar funds JAB's full extraction machine.
Part of JAB's massive fast-casual monopoly. UK staff cuts followed. Prices have risen significantly post-acquisition.

Apollo's $5B buyout loaded Michaels with debt. Preparing for PE exit via re-IPO or sale, prioritizing exit returns over investment. Apollo's $5B LBO of Michaels links it to Leon Black, who paid $158M to Epstein (Dechert LLP, 2021).
Sycamore is a retail-stripping specialist. Closed hundreds of Staples locations and cut workforce repeatedly since 2017.
Sycamore portfolio company. Multiple waves of closures and staff reductions since acquisition.
Apollo drove Claire's to Chapter 11 in 2018. Emerged still burdened with debt. Textbook PE extraction of a teen-focused retailer.
Filed bankruptcy 2020 after years of PE debt-loading. Now owned by former lenders who converted debt to equity. Sweetwater is the independent gold standard.
KKR's third PE owner in a decade. $2.4B in junk-rated debt at acquisition. School athletic programs ultimately absorb higher costs.
Leonard Green drove it to bankruptcy December 2024 — same firm that bankrupted Joann and contributed to Prospect Medical's collapse.

Sycamore completed take-private 2025. Retail-stripping specialist. Mass pharmacy closures expected, reducing access in rural and low-income communities. Pharmacy closures in rural and underserved communities create pharmacy deserts — residents lose their closest source of medications, vaccinations, and clinical services, disproportionately affecting elderly and low-income populations. Sycamore financed the $23.7B buyout with 83% debt ($18.2B), more than double the industry average of 41%, per SEC filings and Private Equity Stakeholder Project analysis.

Apollo took ADT private for $6.9B in 2016, restructured, re-IPO'd 2018. Workers and customers bore debt costs. Apollo retains partial stake. Apollo took ADT private for $6.9B in 2016. Apollo co-founded by Leon Black, who paid $158M to Epstein (Dechert LLP, 2021).
German PE Aurelius acquired 2023, shut all US stores by early 2024. Bankruptcy filed 2024. A brand built on ethics had its ethics immediately put up for sale.

BC Partners' $8.7B LBO was largest of 2014. Controversially transferred Chewy equity outside reach of PetSmart creditors. Apollo added minority stake 2023. Multiple documented incidents of animal deaths and unsanitary conditions in store grooming salons. A 2018 NJ Advance Media investigation found 47 dogs died in PetSmart grooming salons between 2008 and 2018.

CVC loaded Petco with $3.3B in debt. Near-bankruptcy warnings 2023. CEO resigned March 2024. Still majority PE-controlled despite IPO. USDA inspection reports have documented animal welfare violations in Petco stores, including inadequate veterinary care and improper housing conditions for small animals.
Advent's $3B LBO in 2012 ended in Chapter 11 Jan 2023 with $1.9B liabilities. Debt manipulation schemes bankrupted a 90-year-old American brand.
Acquired by Serta Simmons 2018, swept into Advent's bankruptcy Jan 2023 despite being branded as a 'disruptor' mattress line.
Blackstone portfolio company. Premium pricing with ongoing margin extraction via real estate and PE fee strategies.

Apollo owned until 2020 COVID bankruptcy. PE debt burden prevented surviving the economic shock. Classic: load debt, company can't absorb a crisis, workers pay. Children's menu is highly processed with low nutritional value, served in a context that encourages overconsumption through play-and-eat incentives.

Palantir built ICE's case management backbone (FALCON, ICM) beginning in 2013 and in 2025 won a $30M contract to build ImmigrationOS—an AI platform for tracking, targeting, and deporting immigrants. Its software gives thousands of ICE agents access to private and federal databases containing addresses, vehicle records, and biometric data on millions of people.

Clearview AI scraped billions of photos from the internet without consent to build a mass facial recognition database. ICE awarded a $9.2M contract in 2025 to identify and target people in public. ICE began using Clearview in June 2019 before completing the legally required Privacy Impact Assessment—surveillance deployed ahead of any oversight.

GEO Group is ICE's largest private detention contractor with 26,000 beds at 19+ facilities. Its subsidiary BI Incorporated runs ICE's entire electronic monitoring program under a $2.2B sole-source contract. GEO posted record $254M profit in 2025 driven by ICE expansion. Facilities have documented violations including inhumane conditions reported by detainees.

BI Inc., a wholly owned subsidiary of GEO Group, is ICE's sole provider of electronic monitoring under a $2.2B contract. It operates ankle monitors and the SmartLINK smartphone app that surveils 187,000+ immigrants. In December 2025, ICE awarded BI Inc. a $121M skip-tracing contract to locate immigrants who miss check-ins.

CoreCivic operates ICE detention facilities including the 2,400-bed family detention center in Dilley, Texas and new facilities in California, Kansas, and Oklahoma. A 55% increase in immigration contracts in 2025 generated roughly $300M in new ICE revenue. Detainees at the California City facility reported freezing cells, sewage-filled showers, insect infestations, and denied medical care.

Cellebrite's phone-cracking devices are used by ICE to unlock seized phones and extract a complete image of all data including messages, photos, location history, and encrypted app content. ICE renewed an $11M contract and filed notice of intent for a new $35M deal. CBP searched 47,000 devices in 2024, a massive escalation from 8,500 in 2015.

Thomson Reuters' CLEAR platform gives ICE access to license plate records, incarceration data, phone and address records, real estate, credit data, and social media across six contracts totaling $54.4M. Employee unions have protested CLEAR's use for tracking and deporting immigrants with no criminal background. CLEAR data feeds directly into Palantir's ICM system used by ICE agents.

LexisNexis Risk Solutions' Accurint platform holds dossiers on 282 million U.S. residents and gives 11,000+ ICE agents automation tools for vetting, screening, and targeting people for deportation under a $22.1M contract through 2028. A 2023 lawsuit alleged Accurint enables warrantless surveillance. Advocates say ICE uses it as a backdoor around state sanctuary laws.

Motorola Solutions provides ICE with mission-critical tactical communications infrastructure under a $15.6M contract extendable to $28M through 2028. Secure radio and data networks enable ICE enforcement operations in the field. The company also holds $391M+ in Border Patrol radio contracts since 2008.

Amazon Web Services holds 114 federal authorizations to manage DHS data in FY2025—more than any other cloud provider—including ICE case management and biometric databases. AWS Government Cloud hosts the data backbone enabling ICE enforcement operations at scale. Amazon has maintained and expanded these contracts despite internal employee protests.

Accenture Federal Services is the incumbent on ICE's $100M+ cyber defense and intelligence support services contract. It also won a $71M DHS contract to manage an immigration-focused biometric and biographic identity management system. Accenture Federal is one of the largest IT contractors enabling ICE's enforcement and surveillance infrastructure.

GAF is North America's largest roofing manufacturer and a subsidiary of Standard Industries, which was ranked #1 in the PERI UMass Toxic 100 Air Polluters Index (2024 report, 2022 data) — the highest chronic human health risk from air toxic releases of any major U.S. corporation. GAF's asphalt shingle manufacturing plants emit polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs) and other hazardous air pollutants. Frontline communities surrounding GAF plants in Texas, Minnesota, and other states face elevated cancer risks, and residents report years of failed attempts to compel state agencies and the EPA to address the pollution.

Teflon is the world's most recognized non-stick coating brand, manufactured using PFAS ('forever chemicals') by Chemours. PFAS are highly persistent environmental pollutants that accumulate in wildlife and humans. Chemours, which inherited DuPont's PFAS business in 2015, is one of the nation's largest industrial PFAS dischargers. Its plants contaminated drinking water for 70,000+ people near Parkersburg, West Virginia and have since been found to contaminate drinking water for 500,000+ North Carolina residents with GenX PFAS compounds. Chemours ranks among the top 10 U.S. companies by total TRI waste managed (603 million pounds, 2023).

Perdue is one of America's best-known chicken brands, produced by Perdue Farms, which ranked #4 among all U.S. parent companies in the EPA's 2023 Toxics Release Inventory with 2,052 million pounds of chemical waste managed. Industrial poultry processing releases large quantities of ammonia and nitrogen into air and waterways. These emissions contribute to respiratory harm in surrounding rural communities and are a major driver of algal blooms and dead zones in the Chesapeake Bay watershed, where Perdue's operations are concentrated.

Marathon is a major U.S. gasoline retail brand operated by Marathon Petroleum, which was ranked #1 in the PERI UMass Greenhouse 100 Suppliers Index (2022 data) — the largest U.S. supplier of greenhouse-gas-emitting fossil fuel. Marathon Petroleum's 13 refineries are significant EPA Toxics Release Inventory reporters, releasing benzene, volatile organic compounds, and other hazardous chemicals into surrounding communities. As the nation's top petroleum supplier by greenhouse gas contribution, Marathon is a central driver of both local toxic pollution and global climate change.

Exxon is the primary U.S. gasoline brand of ExxonMobil, which ranks in the top 10 of the PERI UMass Greenhouse 100 Polluters index and #5 on the Toxic 100 Water Polluters index (2022 data). ExxonMobil is one of the largest corporate contributors to greenhouse gas emissions globally and a major EPA TRI reporter from its refinery and chemical operations. The company spent decades funding climate denial while internal research showed it understood the science of climate change, making it a central actor in delaying climate action.

Valero is a major U.S. fuel retail brand operated by Valero Energy, the world's largest independent petroleum refiner, ranked #3 in PERI's Greenhouse 100 Suppliers Index (2022 data). Valero's refineries are among the largest EPA Toxics Release Inventory reporters in the country, releasing volatile organic compounds, sulfur dioxide, and benzene into communities near its facilities in Texas, Louisiana, and California. As one of the top U.S. fossil fuel suppliers, Valero's operations are a significant driver of both local toxic pollution and climate change.

Phillips 66 is a major U.S. fuel retail brand ranked #4 in the PERI UMass Greenhouse 100 Suppliers Index (2022 data), making it one of the top U.S. fossil fuel suppliers by greenhouse gas contribution. Phillips 66's refinery operations — including facilities in California, Louisiana, and Texas — are significant EPA TRI reporters, releasing toxic chemicals including benzene, toluene, and sulfur dioxide into surrounding communities. The company also owns the Conoco and 76 fuel brands, giving it one of the largest consumer-facing fuel retail footprints in the U.S.

Texaco is a major U.S. gasoline retail brand now owned by Chevron Corporation, ranked #7 in the PERI UMass Greenhouse 100 Suppliers Index (2022 data). Chevron is one of the largest corporate contributors to global greenhouse gas emissions and a major EPA TRI reporter. Chevron's Richmond, California refinery has caused multiple air quality emergencies, including a 2012 fire that sent approximately 15,000 residents to the hospital. The Texaco brand is also historically associated with catastrophic Amazon oil contamination from legacy Texaco operations in Ecuador that polluted an area the size of Rhode Island.

Cenex is the consumer fuel and lubricant brand of CHS Inc., which was ranked #1 among all U.S. parent companies in the EPA's 2023 Toxics Release Inventory with 4,696 million pounds of chemical waste managed — the highest of any U.S. company. While much of this figure reflects n-hexane recycled from soybean processing, CHS also operates two petroleum refineries whose TRI releases include volatile organic compounds and other hazardous chemicals affecting communities in Montana, Kansas, and across the Midwest and Great Plains.

Brawny is a paper towel brand owned by Georgia-Pacific, a subsidiary of Koch Industries, which ranks in the PERI UMass Toxic 100 Air Polluters top 10. Georgia-Pacific's paper mills are major EPA Toxics Release Inventory reporters. The Green Bay, WI facility alone was responsible for 23% of all industrial discharge in the state (2007-2009). The Crossett, AR mill received a $600,000 EPA fine in 2018 for Clean Air Act violations; Crossett has one of the nation's highest rates of exposure to cancer-causing toxins, with Georgia-Pacific identified as the leading culprit. Koch Industries has amassed 150+ state environmental violation penalties across 20 states.

Duke Energy is one of the highest greenhouse gas emitters of any corporation in the United States, releasing approximately 87 million metric tons of CO2 equivalent per year (2019 EPA data, Greenhouse 100 Polluters Index rank #2). Climate TRACE satellite-verified emissions data corroborates its coal and gas fleet. Duke serves 8.4 million customers and continues to operate coal plants while lobbying against faster clean energy mandates in its service states.

Georgia Power is the primary consumer-facing brand of Southern Company, the third-largest corporate greenhouse gas emitter in the United States (approximately 86 million metric tons CO2e/year, UMass PERI Greenhouse 100 rank #3). Climate TRACE data identifies Southern Company's coal plants in Georgia and Alabama among the highest-emitting power assets in North America. Georgia Power serves 2.7 million customers and has been criticized for slow renewable transition timelines and seeking rate increases to fund nuclear construction overruns.

AEP Energy is the retail electricity brand of American Electric Power, the fifth-largest corporate greenhouse gas emitter in the United States (approximately 70 million metric tons CO2e/year per UMass PERI Greenhouse 100 data, declining to 69.3 MT in 2023). Climate TRACE independently verifies AEP's large fossil fuel generation fleet. AEP serves 5.5 million customers across 11 states and operates the largest high-voltage transmission system in the US, giving it outsized influence over decarbonization pace.

TXU Energy is the retail electricity brand of Vistra Energy, which was the single largest corporate greenhouse gas emitter in the United States in 2019 (approximately 106.5 million metric tons CO2e, UMass PERI Greenhouse 100 rank #1), declining to 86.3 MT in 2023. Climate TRACE satellite data identifies Vistra's Texas coal plants — including Martin Lake and Oak Grove — among the highest-emitting power assets in North America. TXU Energy is one of the largest retail electricity providers in the deregulated Texas market.

Reliant Energy is the primary retail electricity brand of NRG Energy, ranked #11 in the UMass PERI Greenhouse 100 Polluters Index with approximately 36.8 million metric tons of CO2e per year. Climate TRACE tracks NRG's large fossil fuel generation fleet in Texas and the Northeast. Reliant serves hundreds of thousands of Texas households in the deregulated electricity market, marketed under various plans including green energy options — but NRG's core generation remains heavily fossil fuel-dependent.

American Airlines is one of the world's highest-emitting airlines, releasing approximately 45–50 million metric tons of CO2 equivalent annually as tracked by Climate TRACE aviation sector data. It is the world's largest airline by scheduled passengers. American made a net-zero-by-2050 pledge but sustainable aviation fuel accounted for less than 1% of fuel used in 2023 as post-pandemic demand rebounded. Aviation represents one of the hardest-to-decarbonize sectors, and American's continued fleet expansion extends its carbon commitments further into the future.

Delta Air Lines reported scope 1 greenhouse gas emissions of 35.9 million metric tons of CO2 equivalent in 2023 — exceeding its own pre-pandemic 2019 baseline — as tracked by Climate TRACE aviation data and Delta's own ESG report. Delta made a high-profile  billion carbon neutrality pledge in 2020, largely built on offset purchases, which was subsequently challenged by academics and led to a 2023 class-action greenwashing lawsuit. SAF represented less than 0.5% of Delta's fuel in 2023.

United Airlines reported total scope 1 greenhouse gas emissions of approximately 36.6 million metric tons of CO2 equivalent in 2023, placing it among the world's top 10 airline emitters as tracked by Climate TRACE. While United has been more proactive than peers in SAF offtake agreements and contrail-reduction research, SAF represented less than 1% of fuel burned in 2023. United's ongoing international expansion means absolute emissions continue rising even as carbon intensity per seat-mile improves.

Xcel Energy is one of the top 10 corporate greenhouse gas emitters in the United States (approximately 47 million metric tons CO2e/year, UMass PERI Greenhouse 100 rank #7), serving 3.7 million electric customers across eight states. Climate TRACE independently tracks Xcel's coal and gas generation fleet. While Xcel was an early utility to commit to 80% carbon-free generation by 2030, it continued operating large coal plants through 2024 and missed earlier clean energy milestones.

Entergy Corporation ranked #13 in the UMass PERI Greenhouse 100 Polluters Index with approximately 35.8 million metric tons of CO2 equivalent annually, as corroborated by Climate TRACE satellite-verified power sector data. Entergy's service territory in Arkansas, Louisiana, Mississippi, and Texas overlaps heavily with low-income, majority-Black communities who bear disproportionate air quality and climate impacts from its fossil fuel generation. Entergy has faced environmental justice criticism for delaying renewable investment while seeking rate increases.

EWG's Healthy Cleaning Guide rates Tide laundry products as D or F due to 1,4-dioxane — a likely human carcinogen listed by California as a Proposition 65 chemical — found as a contaminant in surfactant ingredients. The New York State Attorney General tested Tide Original in 2019 and found 1,4-dioxane levels exceeding safe thresholds, leading to new state regulations. Tide products also contain optical brighteners and undisclosed synthetic fragrance compounds that can include allergens and hormone-disrupting chemicals. EWG has campaigned for years for P&G to remove 1,4-dioxane from its cleaning formulas.

EWG's Healthy Cleaning Guide rates Febreze air freshener products as D or F, citing undisclosed 'fragrance' formulations that EWG testing and analysis indicates contain volatile organic compounds (VOCs), potential hormone disruptors, and respiratory sensitizers. Febreze products disclose only the generic term 'fragrance' on labels rather than individual chemical ingredients, obscuring potentially hundreds of synthetic chemicals from consumers. EWG analysis of air freshener products found chemicals including acetaldehyde (classified as a possible human carcinogen), limonene (a common sensitizer), and other compounds not disclosed on product labels. EWG rates Febreze poorly for VOC emissions that contribute to indoor air pollution and are released into the atmosphere during use.

EWG's Skin Deep database rates numerous TRESemmé shampoos and conditioners as moderate to high hazard due to DMDM hydantoin — a formaldehyde-releasing preservative linked to hair loss, scalp irritation, and allergic reactions. Multiple class-action lawsuits filed in 2021-2022 alleged TRESemmé products caused significant hair loss and scalp damage due to DMDM hydantoin content. In addition, multiple TRESemmé aerosol dry shampoo products were recalled in late 2021 after the FDA detected benzene — a known human carcinogen — in product samples. Unilever has faced sustained consumer pressure to reformulate these products.

EWG's Skin Deep database rates numerous Dove personal care products — including body washes, shampoos, and antiperspirants — as moderate concern (score 3-6 out of 10) due to synthetic fragrances, BHT (butylated hydroxytoluene, a possible endocrine disruptor), parabens in some product lines, and other ingredients of concern. Dove's 'Real Beauty' marketing positions it as a gentle, consumer-friendly brand, but EWG ingredient analysis reveals the same class of synthetic chemicals found in competitors' products, obscured by 'clean beauty' branding. The brand's aerosol dry shampoo products were also included in the 2021 benzene recall affecting multiple Unilever brands.

EWG's Healthy Cleaning Guide rates Palmolive dish soaps as D or F for containing methylisothiazolinone (MIT) — a preservative EWG rates as high concern for skin sensitization and suspected neurotoxicity — as well as synthetic fragrances and surfactant blends with limited safety data. MIT has been banned or severely restricted in leave-on cosmetics in the European Union due to its known capacity to cause allergic contact dermatitis. The EU Scientific Committee on Consumer Safety has also warned about MIT in rinse-off products. Despite regulatory pressure in Europe, Palmolive products sold in the United States continue to contain MIT in formulations sold as family dish soaps.

EWG's Healthy Cleaning Guide rates Scrubbing Bubbles Disinfectant Bathroom Cleaner as F — its worst grade — primarily for quaternary ammonium compounds ('quats') including alkyl dimethyl benzyl ammonium chloride. The EPA has registered quats as pesticides, and peer-reviewed studies have linked occupational quat exposure to asthma in healthcare workers and cleaning professionals. Animal studies show quats impair fertility and fetal development. EWG also flags the products for synthetic fragrance and chronic toxicity concerns. Despite these issues, Scrubbing Bubbles is marketed aggressively to household consumers cleaning bathrooms and kitchens. Contains quats and chlorinated solvents that persist in wastewater and harm aquatic organisms after household use.

EWG's Healthy Cleaning Guide rates Windex Original Glass Cleaner as D for containing 2-hexoxyethanol — a glycol ether that EWG classifies as high concern for occupational hazard and moderate concern for reproductive toxicity — as well as isopropanolamine, synthetic blue dye, and fragrance compounds not fully disclosed on the label. EWG warns that glycol ethers at high exposure can cause blood disorders and reproductive harm. While household exposure levels may be lower than occupational thresholds, EWG argues consumers deserve to know what they are spraying in enclosed spaces like kitchens and bathrooms.

EWG's Healthy Cleaning Guide rates Lysol Disinfectant Spray and multiple Lysol products as D or F for containing quaternary ammonium compounds ('quats') — EPA-registered pesticides that peer-reviewed research links to occupational asthma, reproductive harm in animal studies, and potential antibiotic resistance. EWG also flags Lysol for undisclosed fragrance formulations and other high-concern ingredients. During the COVID-19 pandemic, Lysol was ubiquitously promoted as a household disinfectant, dramatically increasing consumer and indoor air exposure to quat-containing aerosol sprays — amplifying EWG's public health concerns about chronic low-level exposure. Contains quaternary ammonium compounds that are EPA-registered pesticides; wastewater runoff introduces these biocides into aquatic ecosystems.

EWG's annual Sunscreen Guide has consistently ranked Banana Boat sunscreens among its worst-rated products, citing oxybenzone — a chemical UV filter detected in human blood, urine, and breast milk within minutes of application, and flagged by the FDA as needing further safety data before it can be classified as generally recognized as safe. EWG also found that Hawaii banned oxybenzone-containing sunscreens to protect coral reefs after studies showed it damages coral DNA at parts-per-trillion concentrations. Multiple Banana Boat products also contain retinyl palmitate (vitamin A palmitate), which EWG warns may speed development of skin lesions and tumors when applied to sun-exposed skin, based on U.S. government animal studies. In 2021, Banana Boat recalled multiple aerosol sunscreen products after FDA testing detected benzene contamination.

EWG's annual Sunscreen Guide rates multiple Hawaiian Tropic products poorly due to oxybenzone — a chemical UV filter the FDA has asked manufacturers to provide additional safety data for, and which Hawaii has banned in sunscreens due to documented coral reef damage at trace concentrations. EWG sunscreen analysts also cite inadequate UVA protection in several Hawaiian Tropic formulations relative to their advertised SPF values. High SPF numbers primarily reflect UVB protection; without adequate UVA filters, consumers may experience sunscreen-induced false confidence while incurring cumulative UVA damage. EWG recommends mineral-based sunscreens (zinc oxide, titanium dioxide) as safer alternatives.

Maybelline is owned by L'Oréal, which tests on animals to comply with regulations in mainland China and other markets. PETA lists L'Oréal as a company that tests on animals as of 2024. Maybelline is one of L'Oréal's largest mass-market cosmetics brands, and purchasing it directly funds a parent company that continues to subject animals to safety tests for market access. Source: https://crueltyfree.peta.org/companies-do-test/

Clinique is owned by The Estée Lauder Companies, which confirms it tests on animals 'where required by law,' including in mainland China. PETA lists Estée Lauder as a company that tests on animals. Despite Clinique's marketing as a dermatologist-developed, allergy-tested brand, its parent company continues to subject animals to testing to maintain access to the Chinese cosmetics market. Source: https://crueltyfree.peta.org/companies-do-test/

Rimmel London is owned by Coty Inc., which tests on animals to comply with regulations in markets including mainland China. PETA lists Coty as a company that tests on animals. Rimmel is one of the world's best-selling drugstore makeup brands, and purchasing it funds a parent company that continues to conduct or commission animal testing for regulatory compliance. Source: https://crueltyfree.peta.org/companies-do-test/

OxiClean is owned by Church & Dwight Co., which tests on animals for its household cleaning and personal care products. PETA lists Church & Dwight as a company that tests on animals. Church & Dwight has not obtained cruelty-free certification from any major animal welfare organization. Source: https://crueltyfree.peta.org/companies-do-test/

Schwarzkopf is one of Henkel AG's flagship hair care brands, sold globally including in mainland China. Henkel tests on animals where required by local regulations and appears on PETA's companies-that-test list. Henkel has not obtained cruelty-free certification from Cruelty Free International or PETA. Purchasing Schwarzkopf products funds Henkel's ongoing animal testing practices. Source: https://crueltyfree.peta.org/companies-do-test/

Nivea is the flagship brand of Beiersdorf AG, which sells personal care products worldwide including in mainland China, where animal testing is required for many imported cosmetics. PETA lists Beiersdorf as a company that tests on animals. Beiersdorf has not received cruelty-free certification from Cruelty Free International or PETA. Purchasing Nivea products funds a parent company that continues animal testing for regulatory compliance. Source: https://crueltyfree.peta.org/companies-do-test/

Pine-Sol is owned by The Clorox Company, which tests on animals for its household cleaning products as part of safety and regulatory compliance. PETA lists Clorox as a company that tests on animals. Clorox has not achieved cruelty-free certification from any major animal welfare organization. Source: https://crueltyfree.peta.org/companies-do-test/

Mary Kay Inc. sells personal care and cosmetics products in over 40 countries including mainland China, where animal testing requirements apply to many imported cosmetics. Mary Kay appears on PETA's companies-that-test-on-animals list and has not obtained cruelty-free certification from Cruelty Free International or PETA. Source: https://crueltyfree.peta.org/companies-do-test/

Shiseido Company sells luxury and prestige skincare and cosmetics extensively in mainland China, where animal testing is required for many imported cosmetic products. PETA lists Shiseido as a company that tests on animals. Although Shiseido has pledged to eliminate animal testing, it has not yet achieved cruelty-free certification and continues to test where legally mandated. Source: https://crueltyfree.peta.org/companies-do-test/

Dior Beauty (Parfums Christian Dior) is owned by LVMH, which sells luxury cosmetics and fragrances in mainland China, where animal testing is required for many imported cosmetics. PETA lists LVMH's beauty brands as companies that test on animals. LVMH's decision to prioritize access to the Chinese luxury market means that consumers purchasing Dior Beauty products are indirectly funding animal testing. Source: https://crueltyfree.peta.org/companies-do-test/

Neutrogena, a J&J brand, appears on PETA's list of companies that do not meet cruelty-free standards. J&J sells Neutrogena in China, where imported cosmetics have required government-mandated animal testing. Until J&J restricts sales to markets that mandate animal testing, its consumer brands remain on PETA's 'Does Test on Animals' database.

Aveeno, owned by Johnson & Johnson, is marketed on natural oat-based formulas but falls short on cruelty-free claims. Because J&J distributes Aveeno in China, the brand is subject to animal-testing requirements imposed by Chinese regulators on imported cosmetics. PETA lists Aveeno under companies that do test on animals.

Aquafresh, the toothpaste brand owned by GSK/Haleon, is listed by PETA as a company that conducts animal testing. Oral care products sold in China and other regulated markets are subject to mandatory animal testing requirements, and Aquafresh continues to be sold in these markets without reformulating or withdrawing to avoid testing.

Sensodyne, the sensitive-teeth toothpaste brand under GSK/Haleon, is listed by PETA as conducting animal testing. Sensodyne is sold in mainland China where animal testing is required for imported oral care products. Despite Sensodyne's medical positioning, its parent company has not committed to removing the brand from markets that mandate animal testing.

Revlon is listed by PETA as a company that conducts animal testing on its cosmetics and beauty products. Even after its 2022 bankruptcy restructuring, Revlon continues to sell in markets such as China where animal safety testing is required by regulators for imported cosmetics. PETA maintains Revlon on its 'Does Test on Animals' list.

Avon, now owned by Natura &Co, remains on PETA's list of companies that conduct animal testing. Because Avon sells cosmetics and skincare in China, its products are subject to mandatory pre-market animal testing requirements for imported goods. Despite Natura &Co's sustainability commitments in its other brands, Avon has not obtained cruelty-free certification.

Balenciaga, owned by luxury conglomerate Kering, has fragrance and beauty lines that appear on PETA's list of companies conducting animal testing. Kering-owned fragrance brands use animal-derived musks and conduct regulatory animal testing in markets that require it. Balenciaga's fashion lines also routinely use exotic animal skins and other animal-derived materials.

Blue Buffalo, acquired by Mars, Incorporated in 2018 for $8 billion, is marketed as a premium natural pet food brand but falls short on cruelty-free standards. Mars conducts animal feeding trials and testing on its pet food formulations, and the company's broader track record on animal welfare includes extensive use of animal testing across its pet care portfolio. PETA lists Blue Buffalo's parent Mars as conducting animal testing.

Clarins is a luxury French skincare brand that presents itself as natural and plant-based, yet remains on PETA's 'Does Test on Animals' list. Clarins sells its products in mainland China, where until recently all imported cosmetics required pre-market animal testing by Chinese authorities. By continuing to sell in the Chinese market without meeting cruelty-free standards, Clarins does not qualify for cruelty-free certification.

BIC Razors are manufactured by BIC Corporation, which appears on PETA's list of companies that conduct animal testing. BIC's personal care products—including disposable razors and shaving gels—involve formulations that undergo animal safety testing in some markets. PETA flags BIC Corporation as not meeting cruelty-free standards for its personal care product lines.

Cheerios, made by General Mills, was directly named in EWG's 2024 Cereal Offenders campaign after laboratory testing detected chlormequat — a pesticide linked to reproductive harm in animal studies — at levels above EWG's health benchmark in Cheerios, Honey Nut Cheerios, Oat Crunch Cheerios, and Oat's n' Honey varieties. Chlormequat disrupts the body's hormonal system and has been associated with reduced fertility, delayed puberty, and impaired embryonic growth in animal studies. Despite being marketed as a wholesome breakfast food for children, Cheerios products also contain added sugars and, in some varieties, artificial flavors. EWG's chlormequat study found that 80% of Americans now test positive for the chemical in their bodies, with exposure frequency rising from 69% in 2017 to 90% in 2023.

Quaker Oats, owned by PepsiCo since 2001, was specifically cited in EWG's 2024 chlormequat study: Quaker's Honey Nut Oatmeal Squares and Maple & Brown Sugar Instant Oatmeal both contained chlormequat above 100 parts per billion — exceeding EWG's health benchmark. Chlormequat is a pesticide not approved for use on U.S. food crops but permitted in countries exporting oats to the United States. EWG has also found that Quaker products rank poorly on its Food Scores database due to added sugars, artificial flavors in flavored oatmeal varieties, and high-fructose corn syrup in some granola products. Quaker defended its products by stating they meet all regulatory requirements, but EWG noted that existing EPA tolerances for chlormequat were set without fully accounting for developmental and reproductive effects on children.

Lunchables, marketed by Kraft Heinz and consumed by tens of millions of U.S. children, are rated among the worst products in EWG's Food Scores database. The ham-and-cheese variety contains sodium nitrite — an additive EWG rates as higher concern, linked to increased risk of colorectal cancer in processed meat — along with American pasteurized prepared cheese product and crackers made with enriched bleached flour. Lunchables pizza varieties contain sodium benzoate (moderate concern) while others add artificial food dyes RED 40, BLUE 1, YELLOW 5, and YELLOW 6 (colors linked to hyperactivity in children). All Lunchables kits are classified as NOVA Group 4 ultra-processed foods. Despite being sold in U.S. school cafeterias and marketed to children and parents as a convenient, complete meal, Lunchables provide minimal nutritional value while delivering multiple concerning additives in a single package.

Hot Pockets, manufactured by Nestlé USA since its 2002 acquisition of Chef America, score poorly on EWG's Food Scores database across virtually all varieties. The flagship Pepperoni Pizza Hot Pocket contains sodium nitrite — rated by EWG as a higher-concern ingredient and an established carcinogen in processed meats — along with high sodium levels that can provide 40–50% of the daily adequate sodium intake per serving. Hot Pockets are classified as NOVA Group 4 ultra-processed foods by EWG, containing enriched bleached flour, partially hydrogenated oils (in older formulations), artificial flavors, and mechanically separated chicken and pork in some varieties. The EWG Food Scores database lists Hot Pockets across flavors as scoring in the 7–9 (high concern) range, driven by their sodium content, sodium nitrite, and degree of ultra-processing.

Chef Boyardee canned pasta products, owned by Conagra Brands since 2000, are rated as high-concern on EWG's Food Scores database. The flagship Spaghetti & Meatballs product contains sodium nitrite — an additive EWG rates as higher concern due to its association with increased colorectal cancer risk in processed meat — along with high sodium, refined white pasta, and artificial flavors. All Chef Boyardee products are classified as NOVA Group 4 ultra-processed foods due to their use of multiple industrial ingredients not found in home cooking. Despite their positioning as a children's food and their ubiquity in U.S. pantries, these canned pasta products deliver negligible whole-food nutrition while exposing children to repeated doses of sodium nitrite and high-sodium formulations. EWG's Food Scores methodology specifically flags sodium nitrite as a top-tier concern ingredient that should be avoided.

Oreo cookies, made by Mondelēz International since its 2012 spin-off from Kraft, score poorly on EWG's Food Scores database as a highly ultra-processed snack food. The standard Oreo contains enriched bleached flour, sugar as the second ingredient, high-fructose corn syrup, palm oil, artificial vanilla flavor, and soy lecithin (an emulsifier). EWG rates Oreos as a NOVA Group 4 ultra-processed food with high processing concerns. The product scores in the 7–9 range across varieties due to high sugar content, refined flour, palm oil (with associated deforestation concerns), and artificial flavors. With global sales of over $3.5 billion annually, Oreo is among the world's most widely consumed ultra-processed snack foods, making it a significant contributor to ultra-processed food consumption among children and adolescents.

Fruity Pebbles cereal, made by Post Holdings since 1971, scores among the worst in EWG's Food Scores database for ready-to-eat cereals. The product contains sugar as the second ingredient (after rice), and its distinctive colors come entirely from artificial dyes: RED 40, YELLOW 5, YELLOW 6, and BLUE 1 — petroleum-derived synthetic dyes that EWG flags as moderate-to-higher concern ingredients associated with behavioral problems in children. Multiple EU countries require warning labels on products containing these dyes. Fruity Pebbles is classified as a NOVA Group 4 ultra-processed food, containing refined white rice, BHA (a preservative EWG rates as moderate concern), and artificial fruit flavoring. Despite its marketing to children on Saturday morning television for decades, the cereal provides virtually no nutritional value beyond fortification vitamins added back after processing.

Jif peanut butter, owned by J.M. Smucker since 2002, scores as a moderately concerning product on EWG's Food Scores database. Most Jif varieties — including the flagship Jif Creamy and Jif Extra Crunchy — add sugar and fully hydrogenated vegetable oils as stabilizers, creating a product that EWG rates as more processed than natural single-ingredient peanut butters. Fully hydrogenated oils, while technically trans-fat-free, raise cardiovascular concerns when consumed in quantity.
