EWG's Food Scores specifically flags added sugars and oil additives in peanut butter as nutrition concerns. Jif's parent company, J.M. Smucker, also acquired Hostess Brands in 2023 for $5.6 billion, adding Twinkies, Ding Dongs, and Ho Hos — ultra-processed snack cakes that EWG rates as worst-tier (scores 8–10) due to artificial colors, preservatives, and their classification as NOVA Group 4 foods.

Campbell's condensed soups score poorly on EWG's Food Scores database primarily due to extreme sodium content. A single serving of Campbell's Condensed Chicken Noodle Soup contains approximately 890mg of sodium — 60% of the American Heart Association's recommended 1,500mg daily limit — and a full can provides nearly double the entire daily limit. EWG's Food Scores nutrition methodology flags high sodium as a top-tier concern ingredient given its links to hypertension, cardiovascular disease, and stroke. Campbell's soups also contain artificial flavors and are classified as ultra-processed. Beyond soup, Campbell's Goldfish crackers — one of the most widely consumed children's snacks in the United States — score 6–8 on EWG Food Scores due to enriched bleached flour, artificial coloring in the cheddar variety, and multiple natural flavor additives. Pepperidge Farm cookies (Milano, Chessmen) are also highly processed high-sugar snacks in the same product portfolio.

Pop-Tarts, manufactured by Kellanova (formerly Kellogg's, acquired by Mars Inc. in March 2025 for $35.9 billion), score worst-tier on EWG's Food Scores database. The Froot Loops-flavored Pop-Tarts variety contains artificial colors RED 40 LAKE, RED 40, BLUE 1, and YELLOW 5 — petroleum-derived synthetic dyes that EWG rates as moderate concern and which are banned or require warning labels in several European countries. All Pop-Tarts are classified as NOVA Group 4 ultra-processed foods, containing enriched bleached flour, high-fructose corn syrup, sugar, artificial flavors, and BHT (a preservative of moderate concern). A 2024 social media campaign citing independent research pressured Kellogg's to address the use of artificial dyes, particularly in children's products. Pop-Tarts — with annual U.S. retail sales exceeding $900 million — are one of the most widely consumed ultra-processed breakfast pastries marketed to children in America.

Doritos, PepsiCo's flagship Frito-Lay snack, are classified as NOVA Group 4 ultra-processed food. A standard 1-oz serving of Nacho Cheese Doritos contains MSG, maltodextrin, artificial flavors, and synthetic dyes RED 40, YELLOW 5, and YELLOW 6 — petroleum-derived colorants that EWG flags as moderate concern and that carry mandatory health-warning labels in the EU. EWG's Food Scores database gives Doritos its worst rating due to high sodium, artificial additives, and a heavily refined corn base. A 2024 umbrella review published in The BMJ analyzing 45 meta-analyses found that NOVA Group 4 consumption is associated with a 50% higher risk of cardiovascular mortality, a 12% higher risk of type 2 diabetes, and a 21% higher risk of all-cause mortality. PepsiCo markets Doritos aggressively to teens and young adults through e-sports, music festivals, and social media influencer campaigns, driving annual global sales exceeding $5 billion.

Cheetos, produced by PepsiCo's Frito-Lay division, are rated among the lowest-scoring snack products in EWG's Food Scores database. Crunchy Cheetos contain MSG, artificial cheese flavoring, and YELLOW 6 — a petroleum-based dye associated with hyperactivity in children. Flamin' Hot Cheetos add RED 40 to the dye load. The product's base is degerminated yellow corn meal — a refined grain stripped of fiber — enriched only with synthetic vitamins. All Cheetos varieties are classified as NOVA Group 4 ultra-processed foods per the University of São Paulo classification framework. Multiple studies, including a 2022 JAMA meta-analysis, link regular ultra-processed snack consumption to higher BMI, insulin resistance, and metabolic syndrome in children. Despite documented health concerns, PepsiCo continues to market Cheetos through cartoon mascot Chester Cheetah in campaigns explicitly targeted at children, with annual U.S. retail sales exceeding $1 billion.

Mountain Dew, acquired by PepsiCo in 1964, is one of the most heavily criticized ultra-processed sodas in EWG's database. Original Mountain Dew contains high-fructose corn syrup (170 calories per 12 oz can), brominated vegetable oil (BVO) — a flame retardant–derived additive that the FDA proposed banning in 2023 due to evidence of neurological harm — and Yellow 5 (tartrazine), a synthetic dye that requires health warnings in the EU. In 2020, PepsiCo replaced BVO with glycerol ester of rosin in some formulations, but only after a 200,000-signature consumer petition. Mountain Dew Code Red adds RED 40. EWG rates Mountain Dew a 10/10 (worst tier) for ingredient and processing concerns. A 2021 study in Public Health Nutrition found that daily soda consumption among adolescents — Mountain Dew being the most popular non-cola soda in the U.S. — is independently associated with depression, anxiety, and poor academic performance.

DiGiorno frozen pizza, acquired by Nestlé USA from Kraft in 2010, is classified as a NOVA Group 4 ultra-processed food. A single DiGiorno Pepperoni Pizza serving contains over 700mg of sodium (30% of the recommended daily limit), modified food starch, autolyzed yeast extract, and artificial flavors. The processed meat topping adds sodium nitrite, which IARC classifies as a Group 2A probable carcinogen when consumed regularly as processed meat. EWG's Food Scores database gives DiGiorno's pepperoni varieties a score of 7–9 (high concern) for nutritional and ingredient issues. A 2023 Lancet Regional Health study found that each 10% increase in ultra-processed food calories in the diet raised cardiovascular disease incidence by 6%. Despite these concerns, Nestlé markets DiGiorno as a premium pizza brand with $1.3 billion in annual U.S. sales, positioning it as a convenient family meal.

Stouffer's frozen meals, owned by Nestlé USA since 1973, are classified as NOVA Group 4 ultra-processed foods across virtually their entire product line. Stouffer's Lasagna with Meat & Sauce — a best-seller — contains modified cornstarch, sodium phosphates, carrageenan, and natural flavors (a catch-all category masking undisclosed additives), along with 950mg of sodium per serving (over 40% of daily value). EWG's Food Scores rates most Stouffer's entrées between 7 and 9 (poor tier). The products' reliance on emulsifiers and modified starches — ingredients that studies published in Cell Host & Microbe link to disruption of the gut microbiome — raises particular concern. Stouffer's Macaroni & Cheese contains modified food starch and artificial flavor alongside processed cheese. Nestlé generated approximately $3.7 billion in annual U.S. frozen meal revenue across its brands, with Stouffer's as the flagship, continuing to expand the line despite growing scientific consensus on UPF health risks.

Velveeta, owned by Kraft Heinz since the 2015 merger, is so highly processed it cannot legally be labeled 'cheese' — the FDA requires it to be labeled 'pasteurized prepared cheese product.' Its ingredient list includes milk protein concentrate, sodium citrate, calcium phosphate, sodium phosphate, sorbic acid (preservative), apocarotenal (artificial color), annatto (color), and enzymes. EWG's Food Scores rates Velveeta poorly for nutritional quality and high sodium content (430mg per 1-oz serving). The phosphate additives in Velveeta are of particular scientific concern: a 2012 study in JAMA Internal Medicine found a strong association between high inorganic phosphate intake from food additives and cardiovascular disease mortality, particularly in patients with kidney disease. Velveeta is classified as NOVA Group 4 ultra-processed food due to its industrial processing and multiple additives not found in home cooking. Annual U.S. sales exceed $600 million.

Kool-Aid, marketed by Kraft Heinz (which held the brand via Kraft since 1953), is a powdered drink mix classified as NOVA Group 4 ultra-processed food. Cherry Kool-Aid contains citric acid, calcium phosphate, sodium citrate, artificial flavor, and RED 40 — a petroleum-derived synthetic dye that EWG rates as moderate concern. Grape Kool-Aid adds RED 40 and BLUE 1, while Tropical Punch adds RED 40, BLUE 1, and YELLOW 5. A 2007 double-blind UK Food Standards Agency study — the landmark Southampton Study — found that a mixture of synthetic dyes including RED 40, YELLOW 5, and YELLOW 6 significantly increased hyperactive behavior in children aged 3–9, leading the EU to require warning labels on products containing these additives. Despite decades of controversy over artificial dyes in children's beverages, Kool-Aid continues to market heavily to children through mascot branding and school tie-in programs, with annual U.S. retail sales exceeding $400 million.

Slim Jim, owned by Conagra Brands since 1998, is one of the most heavily criticized ultra-processed meat snacks in EWG's database. A single Slim Jim Original stick contains mechanically separated chicken, pork, sodium nitrite, dextrose, lactic acid starter culture, and hydrolyzed corn and soy protein — a constellation of additives that qualify it as a NOVA Group 4 ultra-processed food. Sodium nitrite, present in Slim Jim, is rated 'avoid' by CSPI (Center for Science in the Public Interest) due to its well-established link to colorectal cancer risk; IARC classifies processed meat consumption as a Group 1 carcinogen. The World Health Organization's 2015 processed meat risk assessment concluded that each 50g daily serving of processed meat increases colorectal cancer risk by 18%. Conagra markets Slim Jim through extreme-sport sponsorships and youth-oriented influencer campaigns while generating over $700 million in annual retail sales across the brand.

Lucky Charms, launched by General Mills in 1964, is consistently rated among the most poorly nutritious children's cereals in EWG's Food Scores database. A standard serving contains 10 grams of sugar (the marshmallow pieces are primarily sugar, modified corn starch, dextrose, and artificial colors RED 40, YELLOW 5, YELLOW 6, and BLUE 1). EWG rates Lucky Charms a 9 out of 10 (worst tier) for nutritional content, citing high sugar, artificial dyes, and heavily processed ingredients. EWG's 2023 Children's Cereal report found that Lucky Charms contains approximately 11 grams of sugar per serving — nearly three times the American Heart Association's recommended daily added sugar limit for children. In 2022, over 4,500 consumers reported gastrointestinal illness after eating Lucky Charms, prompting an FDA investigation into the product's safety. General Mills continues to market Lucky Charms exclusively to children through cartoon mascot advertising despite scientific consensus on the harms of ultra-processed food consumption in childhood.

Chips Ahoy!, owned by Mondelēz International since the 2012 Kraft spin-off, is classified as a NOVA Group 4 ultra-processed food with an EWG Food Scores rating of 8–9 (poor tier). Original Chips Ahoy contains enriched bleached flour (stripped of natural nutrients and fortified with synthetic vitamins), high-fructose corn syrup, artificial flavors, and soy lecithin. Chewy Chips Ahoy adds corn syrup and palm oil. Seasonal and limited editions add RED 40, BLUE 1, and YELLOW 5. The high glycemic index and refined carbohydrate load of Chips Ahoy — alongside its aggressive marketing to children — contributed to Mondelēz's designation by public health researchers as one of the top ultra-processed food companies in a 2023 PLOS ONE analysis of global food corporations' UPF portfolios. Each serving delivers 8–9 grams of sugar with minimal fiber or protein, driving the blood-sugar spikes associated with type 2 diabetes risk in children. Annual U.S. retail sales exceed $700 million.

Jimmy Dean sausage products contain BHA (butylated hydroxyanisole) and sodium nitrite — both rated 'Avoid' by the Center for Science in the Public Interest (CSPI). BHA is classified as a possible carcinogen by California's Office of Environmental Health Hazard Assessment. Sodium nitrite is used as a preservative and color fixative in processed meats; in the stomach it converts to nitrosamines, which the International Agency for Research on Cancer (IARC) classifies as probable human carcinogens, and processed meats overall are classified by IARC as Group 1 carcinogens (sufficient evidence of cancer causation). Jimmy Dean Original Pork Sausage Roll lists both BHA and sodium nitrite on its ingredients panel. Tyson Foods acquired Jimmy Dean from Sara Lee in 2012; the brand generates over $1 billion in annual retail sales.

SPAM Classic's ingredient list includes sodium nitrite — rated 'Avoid' by CSPI — which preserves the meat and gives it its pink color. In the stomach, sodium nitrite reacts with amines in meat to form N-nitrosamines, compounds IARC classifies as probable human carcinogens. Processed meats preserved with nitrite are in IARC Group 1 (causes colorectal cancer). SPAM has been produced by Hormel since 1937 and sells over 150 million cans annually in the U.S. alone. It is one of the most widely recognized processed meat brands globally. CSPI has long called on the FDA to require warning labels on sodium nitrite-containing meats, noting that children who consume them regularly face elevated colorectal cancer risk later in life.

Reese's Peanut Butter Cups contain TBHQ (tert-butylhydroquinone) as an antioxidant preservative. CSPI rates TBHQ as 'Avoid': animal studies showed that high doses caused tumor formation, and it inhibits the process by which immune-system T cells recognize and fight disease. TBHQ is among the most common preservatives in American packaged foods — found in pop-tarts, crackers, fast-food frying oils, and candy. The FDA permits TBHQ at concentrations up to 0.02% of the oil content of a product. Reese's is the top-selling candy brand in the United States with annual retail sales exceeding $2.8 billion. In 2023, Hershey also settled class-action litigation alleging its dark chocolate products contained undisclosed lead and cadmium levels exceeding California Prop 65 safe harbor thresholds.

Diet Coke contains two additives rated as concerns by CSPI: acesulfame potassium (Ace-K), rated 'Avoid' because inadequate safety testing conducted in the 1970s included one study with a modest increased cancer signal in mice, and aspartame, rated 'Certain people should avoid' (phenylalanine metabolized from aspartame can be harmful to phenylketonurics; CSPI has long called for better labeling). Diet Coke also contains phosphoric acid (associated with reduced bone density at high intakes) and caramel color, whose manufacturing byproduct 4-methylimidazole (4-MEI) prompted California's Prop 65 cancer warning for Coca-Cola products in 2012. Diet Coke is one of the three best-selling carbonated soft drinks in the United States with annual retail sales exceeding $3 billion.

Froot Loops contains Red 40, Blue 1, and Yellow 6 — all synthetic food dyes rated 'Avoid' by CSPI. A 2007 double-blind, randomized, placebo-controlled study published in The Lancet (McCann et al.) found that mixtures of artificial food colorings including Red 40, Yellow 6, and others caused a statistically significant increase in hyperactivity in children across multiple age groups. The European Union responded by requiring warning labels on products with these dyes stating: 'May have an adverse effect on activity and attention in children.' The U.S. FDA has not required such labeling, relying on an advisory committee review in 2011 that found the existing evidence inconclusive — though CSPI had filed a petition to ban these dyes as early as 2008. Froot Loops generates approximately $400 million in annual U.S. retail sales.

Hawaiian Punch Fruit Juicy Red contains Red 40 and Blue 1 — artificial colors rated 'Avoid' by CSPI. Red 40 is the most widely used food dye in the United States; CSPI notes it may contain p-Cresidine, a compound the U.S. National Toxicology Program has found to cause tumors in mice, and that it causes hypersensitivity (allergic-type) reactions in some people. Blue 1 may carry a small risk of cancer and has not been conclusively proven safe. Hawaiian Punch is marketed heavily to children and generates over $1 billion in annual U.S. retail sales. Despite containing less than 5% actual juice, it is sold as a 'fruit punch' product. Keurig Dr Pepper acquired Hawaiian Punch from Procter & Gamble via the 1999 sale that eventually passed to Cadbury Schweppes.

Wonder Bread contains calcium propionate — an antimold preservative CSPI rates as 'Certain people should avoid' — and DATEM (diacetyl tartaric acid esters of mono- and diglycerides), a dough conditioner CSPI also rates as 'Certain people should avoid' due to insufficient long-term safety data. A 2002 controlled study by Dengate and Ruben (Journal of Paediatrics and Child Health) found calcium propionate caused irritability, restlessness, and inattention in some children. A subsequent 2019 Australian study linked propionic acid (the active antimicrobial compound in calcium propionate) to autism-related behavioral changes in animal models, though this link remains under investigation. Wonder Bread is one of the most recognized bread brands in America, with a history dating to 1921. Flowers Foods acquired it from Hostess for $360 million in 2012.

Crisco All-Vegetable Shortening lists TBHQ (tert-butylhydroquinone) as an antioxidant ingredient. CSPI rates TBHQ as 'Avoid': animal studies showed tumor formation at high doses, and mechanistic research indicates TBHQ impairs T-cell immune function. TBHQ is also present in Crisco vegetable oil products. Originally introduced by Procter & Gamble in 1911, Crisco was later sold to J.M. Smucker in 2002 and subsequently to B&G Foods in 2020 for $550 million. In its original formulation, Crisco was a significant source of artificial trans fats (partially hydrogenated oils), which were banned by the FDA in 2015 and phased out by 2018. The reformulated version eliminated trans fats but retained TBHQ as a stabilizer. Crisco is the dominant U.S. shortening brand, used in baking and frying by millions of households.

Thomas' Original English Muffins contain calcium propionate (an antimold preservative) and DATEM (a dough conditioner) — both rated 'Certain people should avoid' by CSPI. Calcium propionate has been linked in controlled studies to irritability and inattention in some children (Dengate & Ruben, 2002), and a 2019 study in Scientific Reports found propionic acid triggered autism-relevant behavioral and metabolic changes in rodents. DATEM lacks long-term safety data from independent studies. Thomas' English Muffins are one of the best-selling bread products in the United States, with annual retail sales exceeding $500 million. Grupo Bimbo acquired Thomas' as part of its 2011 purchase of Bimbo Bakeries USA from Sara Lee Corporation for $959 million.

Nerds candy (original variety) contains Red 40, Blue 1, Yellow 5 (tartrazine), and Yellow 6 — four artificial dyes CSPI rates as 'Avoid.' The McCann et al. (2007) Lancet study found that mixtures including these exact dyes significantly increased hyperactivity in 3-year-olds and 8–9-year-olds in a double-blind trial. Yellow 5 (tartrazine) has additionally been linked to hyperactivity and allergic reactions including urticaria and asthma in sensitive individuals. Yellow 6 contains trace amounts of carcinogenic contaminants (benzidine and 4-aminobiphenyl). The EU requires warning labels on all products containing these dyes stating they 'may have an adverse effect on activity and attention in children.' Nerds are one of the top-selling candies in the United States, with annual retail sales estimated at $500 million+. Ferrara Candy (maker of Nerds) was acquired by Ferrero Group in 2017 for approximately $1.3 billion.

Bath & Body Works is the surviving consumer brand of L Brands, whose founder Les Wexner granted Jeffrey Epstein power of attorney over his financial empire for years. Unsealed court documents name Wexner over 1,000 times in connection with Epstein and Virginia Giuffre's testimony alleges Epstein sexually trafficked her to Wexner. Wexner was deposed by Congress in February 2026 on his role in enabling Epstein's crimes.

Unsealed Epstein court documents establish that Jeffrey Epstein systematically used the Victoria's Secret brand as a lure, claiming to be a talent scout for the company to recruit young women into his trafficking network. The brand was owned by L Brands under Les Wexner, who gave Epstein power of attorney over his financial holdings. Victoria's Secret was spun off as an independent company in 2021 but its origins are inseparable from this documented history.

Chase is the consumer-banking arm of JPMorgan Chase & Co., which paid $290 million to Epstein survivors and $75 million to the U.S. Virgin Islands after court documents showed the bank knowingly facilitated Epstein's sex-trafficking operation for 15 years. Internal records unsealed in 2023-2025 document over $1 billion in suspicious transactions the bank processed through Epstein-linked accounts while its compliance officers' concerns were repeatedly overruled by senior executives.

Deutsche Bank maintained Jeffrey Epstein's accounts from 2013 to 2018 — years after his 2008 sex-offender conviction — despite repeated internal red flags. The New York Department of Financial Services fined the bank $150 million in 2020 for compliance failures that enabled Epstein's continued financial operations. In 2023 Deutsche Bank paid $75 million to settle claims from Epstein survivors, with court documents confirming the bank processed payments used in his trafficking network.

Barclays CEO Jes Staley (2015-2021) had a deeply documented personal and professional relationship with Jeffrey Epstein. Court proceedings produced over 1,000 emails between them; Staley called Epstein 'family' and visited his private island. The UK Financial Conduct Authority banned Staley from financial services for life in 2025 for misleading Barclays' board and UK regulators about the nature of this relationship — a direct accountability failure at the top of a major consumer bank.

Highbridge Capital co-founder Glenn Dubin is named in Virginia Giuffre's deposition released in January 2024, which alleges he was the first person Ghislaine Maxwell directed her to have sex with. Epstein invested millions in Highbridge and helped broker JPMorgan's acquisition of the fund. Flight logs show Dubin flew on Epstein's plane at least 18 times between 1996 and 2005.

MC2 Model Management was co-founded and seed-funded by Jeffrey Epstein's $1 million investment circa 2005, operated by Jean-Luc Brunel. Court documents from the Maxwell civil case allege MC2 was a primary vehicle in Epstein's trafficking network, using modeling contracts as cover to supply Epstein with young women. Brunel was arrested in France in 2020 on rape and trafficking charges; the agency sold off its assets after his arrest.

Carbyne received direct co-investment from Jeffrey Epstein through his Southern Trust company as part of a documented business partnership with former Israeli PM Ehud Barak. Leaked emails confirm Epstein was using the Carbyne investment to broker connections in the surveillance industry to powerful political and intelligence figures. The company markets emergency-dispatch software to governments and municipalities.

D.E. Shaw hired Larry Summers as a marquee hire in 2006, one year after Summers flew on Epstein's private jet for a honeymoon trip to Epstein's Caribbean island while serving as Harvard president. Flight records document four Epstein aircraft flights by Summers during his Harvard tenure. D.E. Shaw maintained the relationship for two decades before severing it in 2026 after leaked emails revealed the full scope of Summers' documented association with Epstein.

Bank of America agreed to a $72.5 million settlement with Jeffrey Epstein accusers in March 2026, becoming the third major US bank to settle Epstein-related trafficking facilitation claims after JPMorgan Chase and Deutsche Bank. Litigation documents allege the bank processed payments connected to Epstein's network and failed to act on anti-money-laundering obligations despite red flags.

Fox News is owned by Fox Corporation, Rupert Murdoch's media empire. In 2023, Fox Corp paid $787.5 million — the largest US media defamation settlement in history — after a jury was set to hear evidence that Fox News hosts and executives knowingly broadcast false claims about the 2020 election to protect ratings. LittleSis maps the Murdoch family's direct access to heads of government across three countries and shows how that relationship has been used to shape regulatory and policy decisions in Fox's commercial interest, with no individual held accountable for the systematic deception.

The Venetian Resort in Las Vegas is the flagship property of Las Vegas Sands Corp, founded by Sheldon Adelson. The Adelson family leveraged casino profits to build one of the largest dark money political machines in US history, spending over $200 million to elect politicians who then shaped legislation on drug policy, Israel policy, and antitrust matters directly benefiting LVS. LittleSis documents the feedback loop between Adelson donations and favorable government access, a textbook example of wealth purchasing policy impunity.

The Wynn and Encore resorts in Las Vegas are operated by Wynn Resorts, whose founder Steve Wynn resigned in 2018 amid 200+ sexual misconduct allegations and later was indicted in 2023 for acting as an unregistered foreign agent for the Chinese government. Wynn's elite connections — as RNC Finance Chair and a prominent Republican donor — gave him access to White House officials he exploited to lobby for immigration policy changes favoring Chinese nationals. The company paid $26.7 million in settlements but continues to operate under his name, profiting from the brand identity he built.

The New York Post is owned by News Corp, Rupert Murdoch's publishing conglomerate. The Post has operated as a vehicle for political influence and tabloid misinformation for decades, with Murdoch using it to reward allies and punish opponents. News Corp's UK outlets were convicted in the phone hacking scandal that led to criminal convictions, shuttering of News of the World, and over $1.6 billion in civil settlements. LittleSis documents how Murdoch's editorial control across his outlets has historically translated into direct policy access — UK prime ministers have sought his blessing before elections, and US officials have been documented responding to Murdoch's coverage preferences.

Oracle is an enterprise software company where founder Larry Ellison holds ~40% of shares and exercises dominant control. LittleSis documents how Ellison's political fundraising — including a 2019 event for President Trump — correlated with favorable government treatment: the White House intervened in 2020 to steer the TikTok US deal to Oracle over a stronger competing bid from Microsoft. Oracle has also received hundreds of millions in federal contracts while Ellison maintained direct access to officials. Ellison's purchase of the Hawaiian island of Lanai has drawn scrutiny for water rights practices that have harmed local communities with minimal state accountability.

Anduril Industries, backed by Peter Thiel's Founders Fund, has secured over $1.5 billion in Pentagon contracts for autonomous weapons and surveillance systems with limited public oversight of its AI-driven lethality decisions. Its Lattice border surveillance platform is deployed at the US-Mexico border for immigration enforcement. LittleSis documents Anduril's position within the Thiel political network, whose members have funded efforts to weaken democratic oversight of AI weapons, funded election denialism, and secured government contracts through political access rather than competitive merit — part of a broader pattern of private defense tech firms concentrating power outside normal accountability structures.

Angi (formerly Angie's List / HomeAdvisor) is controlled by IAC/InterActiveCorp, the media empire of Barry Diller. Angi faced class action litigation for billing contractors for fraudulent leads generated by fake accounts, a practice the FTC later investigated. IAC's broader pattern of acquiring platforms and extracting value through dark patterns and deceptive billing reflects the elite access Diller's network provides: decades of regulatory deference enabled IAC to build a dominant home-services marketplace while sidelining accountability. LittleSis documents Diller's bipartisan donor relationships that have created a protective buffer around his business operations.

Warner Music Group — home to Atlantic Records, Warner Records, and Elektra — is majority controlled by Access Industries, the holding company of Len Blavatnik. LittleSis documents Blavatnik's documented business history with Viktor Vekselberg, a sanctioned Russian oligarch, through their joint venture TNK-BP. While Vekselberg faced US sanctions, Blavatnik — who became a US citizen and major political donor — avoided similar scrutiny. His $1 million donation to Trump's inaugural committee and substantial contributions to bipartisan politicians have provided a layer of political protection that his oligarch-era associates did not enjoy.

Ticketmaster, merged with Live Nation in 2010, operates a documented monopoly over primary and secondary concert ticketing. The DOJ's 2024 antitrust lawsuit filed alongside 30 state AGs found Live Nation/Ticketmaster used exclusionary contracts, retaliation against venues, and market power to suppress competition for over a decade. LittleSis documents how Live Nation's lobbying network delayed DOJ action for years after regulators first identified concerns at the 2010 merger. The result: consumers faced billions in junk fees, artists lost negotiating leverage, and the company used political access to avoid the antitrust enforcement that market behavior clearly warranted.

Dixie disposable cups, plates, and cutlery are manufactured by Georgia-Pacific, a subsidiary of Koch Industries. The Koch brothers have used their industrial fortune to build one of the most powerful dark money networks in American history — funding ALEC (American Legislative Exchange Council), Americans for Prosperity, and dozens of astroturf organizations that write and lobby for legislation immunizing polluters, weakening labor protections, and blocking climate regulation. LittleSis documents how this network has provided Koch Industries itself with regulatory protection, including decades of documented environmental violations at Koch-owned facilities that resulted in far lighter penalties than comparable violations by companies without such political infrastructure.

Goldman Sachs helped orchestrate the 1MDB scandal — the largest kleptocracy in Malaysian history — raising $6.5B in bonds for a sovereign wealth fund while its subsidiary paid $1.6B in bribes to secure the business. Goldman's Malaysian subsidiary pleaded guilty in 2020; the bank paid $2.9B to US authorities and $3.9B globally. ICIJ's FinCEN Files documented Goldman Sachs processing suspicious transactions flagged in hundreds of FinCEN Suspicious Activity Reports. The bank's ability to negotiate deferred prosecution for most of its parent entities — while a subsidiary took the criminal plea — illustrates how elite financial institutions navigate accountability that would land others in prison.

HSBC's Swiss private banking arm was exposed by ICIJ's SwissLeaks as systematically helping 30,000 clients in 200 countries hide ~$100B from tax authorities — including arms dealers, blood diamond traffickers, and politicians under investigation. HSBC paid $1.9B in 2012 for laundering money for the Sinaloa cartel and Juárez cartel, and for processing transactions for sanctioned regimes. Despite its criminal conduct, HSBC received a deferred prosecution agreement — no criminal conviction — because US regulators concluded the bank was 'too big to jail.' This two-tier justice system, where a major bank faces fines while individuals go to prison for far lesser offenses, exemplifies elite financial impunity.

IQOS is Philip Morris International's heated tobacco product, sold in the US since 2019. PMI was documented in ICIJ's Paradise Papers using offshore Netherlands structures to shelter profits and funding covert third-party lobbying campaigns through offshore entities to undermine tobacco plain packaging laws in Australia and the UK. Internal documents showed PMI used law firms in offshore jurisdictions to conceal the company's involvement from regulators. PMI simultaneously markets IQOS as a harm-reduction product while using the same offshore financial engineering exposed in the Paradise Papers to fund political campaigns protecting its tobacco revenue streams.

Glencore is the world's largest commodity trader, documented in ICIJ's Panama Papers as using Mossack Fonseca offshore shell companies to obscure deal-making and beneficial ownership in resource transactions across Africa and Latin America. In 2022 Glencore pleaded guilty to bribery charges across the US, UK, and Brazil — paying $1.5B — after DOJ documented it paying $100M+ in bribes to officials in seven countries including the DRC, Nigeria, and Venezuela to secure oil access. The DOJ described a company culture where bribery was institutionalized. Glencore's offshore architecture, exposed in the Panama Papers, was integral infrastructure for maintaining its impunity.

Credit Suisse's private banking arm, exposed by ICIJ's Suisse Secrets investigation (2022), held accounts for convicted human rights abusers, sanctioned oligarchs, dictators' families, and a Honduran drug lord — accounts whose continued existence violated Credit Suisse's own policies. The bank paid $2.6B in 2014 for helping American clients hide assets from the IRS and pleaded guilty — a rare criminal guilty plea for a major bank. Despite the conviction, senior executives faced no personal prosecution. Credit Suisse continued to acquire offshore clients after its US guilty plea, and the Suisse Secrets leak showed the behavior was systemic rather than isolated. The bank's collapse in 2023 — triggered by a cascade of scandals including the Archegos fund blowup — did not result in personal criminal accountability for any executive.

Altice USA — which serves ~9 million US cable and broadband customers under the Optimum and Suddenlink brands — is ultimately controlled by billionaire Patrick Drahi through British Virgin Islands shell companies documented in ICIJ's Pandora Papers (2021). While building a telecom empire requiring US regulatory approvals and serving US consumers, Drahi structured his ownership through BVI entities to reside in Switzerland and minimize personal taxes across jurisdictions. Consumer complaints about Suddenlink/Optimum service quality and junk fees have generated some of the worst customer satisfaction scores in the US cable industry; the company has faced state AG investigations into deceptive billing while its offshore ownership structure limited shareholder accountability.

ING Bank, documented in ICIJ's Paradise Papers for offshore tax structures, paid €775M in 2018 — one of the largest corporate criminal settlements in Dutch history — after Dutch prosecutors found ING had enabled systemic money laundering for a decade. Corporate clients used ING accounts to pay millions in bribes to obtain contracts in Russia and the Netherlands Antilles, including payments connected to an oil deal involving a company linked to a sanctioned entity. Prosecutors found that senior management was aware of compliance failures and failed to act. ING served tens of thousands of retail consumers in the Netherlands and Europe while its wholesale banking arm processed these transactions without adequate controls.

Société Générale was named in ICIJ's Panama Papers as having more Mossack Fonseca-linked shell company connections than any other French bank — over 1,000 entities — facilitating offshore structures for clients who subsequently faced French tax investigations. In 2018 SocGen paid €963M to French authorities for tax fraud and approximately $860M to US authorities for FCPA bribery violations in Libya and LIBOR manipulation. The Libya bribery scheme involved payments to a fund controlled by Libyan officials in exchange for $3.66B in investment mandates. SocGen served as the deal's arranger while routing payments through third-party intermediaries — a structure the DOJ said was designed to conceal bribery.

BNP Paribas pleaded guilty in 2014 and paid $8.9B — the largest criminal fine ever imposed on a bank at the time — for processing $8.9B in transactions for customers in Sudan, Iran, and Cuba in violation of US sanctions. DOJ found BNP employees deliberately removed identifying information ('stripping') from wire transfers to hide the transactions from US correspondent banks over nearly a decade. Employees who raised concerns were sidelined. Despite the guilty plea, senior executives escaped personal criminal charges. ICIJ's Panama Papers also documented BNP Paribas subsidiaries maintaining offshore shell company structures for clients through Mossack Fonseca. BNP Paribas operates retail banking and wealth management services for millions of customers globally.

Novartis was documented in ICIJ's Paradise Papers using Bermuda and Jersey offshore structures to hold pharmaceutical intellectual property, routing royalties through low-tax jurisdictions on drugs sold at premium prices in high-tax markets — including the US — partly justified by claimed R&D investment. In 2019 Novartis paid $678M to settle DOJ charges that it paid kickbacks through 'speaker programs' — sham medical education events — to US doctors to prescribe its drugs. In the same year, it priced its gene therapy Zolgensma at $2.1M per dose while facing Swiss criminal investigations for data manipulation in the drug's clinical trials. The combination of offshore profit sheltering and domestic physician bribery, while pricing life-saving drugs beyond reach, illustrates the systemic accountability gap the ICIJ investigations documented.

Booz Allen Hamilton holds a $51.9M contract with ICE to design, develop, and provide data analytics for the RAVEN data repository used by ICE Homeland Security Investigations agents. RAVEN centralizes investigative data enabling agents to identify and target individuals for immigration enforcement. Documented by Fortune's 2025 investigation of Fortune 500 companies with active ICE contracts (contract period through September 2025).

CACI International holds a $119.9M contract (potential $130.6M) with ICE for tactical communications operations and maintenance — the field radio and communications infrastructure that ICE agents rely on during enforcement operations, arrests, and deportation activities. Contract runs through March 2026. Documented by Fortune's 2025 investigation of Fortune 500 companies with active ICE contracts.

AT&T holds an $83M contract (potential $164.5M with extensions to 2032) with ICE for IT, network products, and support services — providing the network infrastructure that underpins ICE enforcement operations. Millions of Americans pay monthly AT&T wireless and broadband bills while the company simultaneously provides core infrastructure to ICE. Documented by Fortune's 2025 investigation of Fortune 500 companies with active ICE contracts.

Deloitte's U.S. federal division holds multiple active ICE contracts. The PERC contract (award ID 70CDCR24FR0000002, ~$46.2M) provides 24/7 real-time enforcement intelligence to 17 ICE field offices across 42 states — the Pacific Enforcement Response Center processes targeting intelligence for immigration arrests nationwide. Deloitte also holds an ~$50M task order for Homeland Security Investigations analytical support and an ~$24M data modernization contract awarded September 2023. Source: OrangeSlices AI analysis of USASpending data; Documented investigative reporting, February 2026.

Amentum Services received a no-bid $453M ICE contract (effective March 2026, CONT_AWD_70CDCR25FR0000062) to operate Camp East Montana at Fort Bliss in El Paso, Texas — the nation's largest ICE detention facility with capacity for ~5,000 detainees. Amentum replaced prior operator Acquisition Logistics LLC, whose $1.2B contract was terminated after reports of 49 detention standard violations and in-custody deaths. The contract covers detention, transportation, and medical services through September 2026. Source: USASpending.gov; El Paso Matters, March 2026; KFOX-TV.

LaSalle Corrections is a family-owned private detention operator (Ruston, Louisiana) with 18 ICE detention facilities across Louisiana, Texas, and Georgia — total capacity over 13,000 detainees. In 2025 LaSalle received a contract of up to $125M to build a new ICE detention facility. A $7M wrongful death settlement (2024) and multiple ACLU lawsuits document inadequate medical care and unsafe conditions at LaSalle-operated ICE facilities. Source: Facing South analysis of USASpending data, December 2025; ICE.gov; Prison Legal News.

Cup Noodles, manufactured by Nissin Foods Holdings, is classified as a NOVA Group 4 ultra-processed food under the NOVA classification system (Monteiro et al., 2019, PMC6322572) — the most processed category, defined by products manufactured from extracted or modified food substances plus additives rarely used in home kitchens. A single serving of Cup Noodles (original) contains 1,190 mg sodium (52% of the daily recommended value), TBHQ (a preservative rated “Avoid” by CSPI’s Chemical Cuisine due to possible carcinogenicity and vision disturbances at high doses), MSG, sodium phosphate, disodium guanylate, and disodium inosinate. A 2014 Harvard School of Public Health study (Oh et al., Journal of Nutrition) found women consuming instant noodles two or more times per week faced a 68% higher risk of metabolic syndrome independent of overall diet. With more than 40 billion Cup Noodles units sold annually worldwide and significant marketing to budget-constrained and college-age consumers, the product represents one of the most widely distributed NOVA Group 4 foods in the United States.

Eckrich is a processed meat brand owned by Smithfield Foods (WH Group), producing smoked sausages, bologna, hot dogs, corn dogs, and breakfast sausages sold in virtually every US supermarket. All Eckrich meat products list sodium nitrite as a preservative and color fixative. CSPI's Chemical Cuisine rates sodium nitrite as 'Avoid' — its highest-concern rating — because it reacts with amines in the digestive tract to form N-nitrosamines, compounds classified as probable human carcinogens by IARC. In October 2015, IARC upgraded processed meats to Group 1 carcinogenic to humans (sufficient evidence of cancer in humans), specifically citing colorectal cancer risk. The WHO's analysis found that eating 50 grams of processed meat daily — roughly two Eckrich sausages — increases colorectal cancer risk by approximately 18%. ConAgra sold Eckrich to Smithfield Foods in October 2006 for approximately $575 million.

Costco pays starting wages of $19.50/hour (as of 2023), provides health benefits to part-time workers after 180 days, and has one of the lowest employee turnover rates in retail. Its employee-focused model is a direct contrast to race-to-the-bottom retail labor practices. Source: Costco investor relations and wage announcements (costco.com/jobs.html).

Patagonia pays all employees a living wage, offers on-site childcare at its California headquarters, and since 2022 is 98% owned by the Holdfast Collective nonprofit whose sole purpose is fighting climate change. The company is also a founding member of 1% for the Planet, donating 1% of all sales to environmental nonprofits. Source: patagonia.com/our-footprint/

REI is a consumer cooperative where members receive profit-share dividends and employees earn living wages with profit-sharing and generous benefits including gear discounts and outdoor adventure subsidies. REI consistently ranks on Fortune's '100 Best Companies to Work For.' Source: rei.com/stewardship

Dr. Bronner's pays all US employees at least $24/hour, maintains a strict 5:1 executive pay ratio (highest to lowest paid), and donates roughly 50% of annual profits to social causes. These policies are documented in their annual All-One! Report. Source: drbronner.com/all-one/cause-engagement/fair-pay/

In 2015, CEO Dan Price cut his own $1.1M salary to $70,000 to fund a $70,000 minimum wage for all Gravity Payments employees. The policy reduced employee financial stress and cut turnover in half. By 2023, the minimum salary had risen to $80,000. Source: gravitypayments.com/blog/team/

WinCo Foods is employee-owned, with workers participating in a retirement fund that has accumulated over $650 million in assets. Grocery clerks earn competitive wages and the company has a 50-year track record of sharing profits with employees rather than outside shareholders. Source: wincofoods.com/company-info

Publix employees own approximately 80% of the company's stock through a profit-sharing plan available after one year of employment. The chain has appeared on Fortune's '100 Best Companies to Work For' every year since 1998. Source: corporate.publix.com/about-publix/company-overview

King Arthur Baking Company is 100% employee-owned via an ESOP (since 2004) and is a certified B Corporation. Employees share in company profits and participate in company governance. Source: kingarthurbaking.com/learn/about-king-arthur

Eileen Fisher has transferred over 30% of company ownership to employees through its Leadership Awards program, making it one of the largest women-led businesses to adopt meaningful employee ownership. The company also offers profit-sharing and pays living wages throughout its supply chain. Source: eileenfisher.com/pages/social-consciousness-employee-ownership

Dannon is part of Danone North America, the world's largest certified B Corporation (since 2018). Danone North America has committed to paying all US employees a living wage as defined by MIT's Living Wage Calculator and publishes annual B Corp impact reports detailing worker welfare metrics. Source: danonenorthamerica.com/whats-new/danone-north-america-certified-as-b-corporation/

Allbirds prints a carbon footprint label on every product, is a certified B Corporation, and uses sustainable materials (merino wool, eucalyptus fiber, sugarcane). As of 2023, Allbirds offsets 100% of its remaining carbon emissions while working to eliminate them. Source: allbirds.com/pages/sustainability

Ecosia uses 80%+ of search ad revenue to plant trees, has financed over 200 million trees across 35 countries, and runs on 100% renewable energy. Its articles of association legally prevent profit extraction, locking in the environmental mission. Source: blog.ecosia.org/ecosia-financial-reports-tree-planting-receipts/

Who Gives A Crap makes plastic-free toilet paper from 100% recycled fiber or FSC-certified bamboo, and donates 50% of profits to sanitation causes. As of 2023, the company has donated over $13 million to build toilets globally and is a certified B Corporation. Source: au.whogivesacrap.org/pages/about-us

Tentree plants 10 trees per item purchased, is a certified B Corporation, and targets planting 1 billion trees by 2030. Customers receive a unique code to track the specific trees planted from their purchase. As of 2023, the company has planted over 100 million trees. Source: tentree.com/pages/impact

Pela Case makes phone cases from compostable bioplastic (Flaxstic from flax straw waste), is a certified B Corporation, and runs a take-back program (Pela 360) to compost returned cases. Products are certified compostable and the company is working toward fully plastic-free operations by 2028. Source: pelacase.com/pages/sustainability

Grove Collaborative is a certified B Corporation and was the first plastic-neutral retailer in the US (2021). It bans over 130 harmful chemicals from its product catalog and is committed to going 100% plastic-free by 2025. Source: grove.co/about

Blueland's tablet-based cleaning system eliminates single-use plastic bottles — customers buy a reusable bottle once and refill with dissolvable tablets. A certified B Corporation, Blueland estimates it has prevented over 1 billion single-use plastic bottles as of 2023. Source: blueland.com/pages/our-mission

Cotopaxi is a certified B Corporation, 1% for the Planet member, and uses recycled/remnant fabrics in product lines like Del Dia. The company donates 1%+ of revenue to its foundation focused on poverty alleviation and environmental protection. Source: cotopaxi.com/pages/our-mission

Numi Organic Tea is USDA Organic, Fair Trade certified, a certified B Corporation, and carbon-neutral. Tea bags are plastic-free and compostable. The company publishes an annual impact report detailing environmental and worker impact metrics. Source: numitea.com/values/

Preserve makes toothbrushes, razors, and kitchen products from 100% recycled plastics (including #5 polypropylene rarely accepted by curbside programs) and takes back products at end of life for recycling. The company has pioneered closed-loop manufacturing since 1996 and partners with Stonyfield Organic to upcycle yogurt cups into toothbrushes. Source: preserveproducts.com/about/

Lush is Leaping Bunny certified, refuses to sell in markets requiring animal testing (including mainland China), and makes approximately 50% vegan products. The company actively funds campaigns for a global animal testing ban, including the 'Fighting Animal Testing' campaign. Source: lushusa.com/stories/article.lush-ethical-buying-policy.html

e.l.f. Cosmetics is 100% vegan and Leaping Bunny certified — no animal testing at any stage of production, including by suppliers. It was among the first major cosmetics brands to achieve full vegan status across all products and has maintained this commitment since 2004. Source: elfcosmetics.com/cruelty-free-vegan-cosmetics.html

Pacifica Beauty products are certified vegan (Vegan Action) and Leaping Bunny certified cruelty-free across the entire product line — cosmetics, skincare, and fragrance. No animal testing at any stage. Source: pacificabeauty.com/pages/clean-beauty

Derma E has been 100% vegan and cruelty-free since 1984, holding both PETA and Leaping Bunny certifications. No animal ingredients or animal testing at any supply chain stage. One of the oldest continuously vegan skincare brands in the US. Source: dermae.com/pages/dermae-values

100% PURE products are certified vegan and cruelty-free, pigmented with fruit and vegetable extracts instead of synthetic dyes, and formulated with no animal-derived ingredients. The brand has maintained full vegan certification since founding in 2004. Source: 100percentpure.com/pages/our-story

Meow Meow Tweet is 100% vegan (Vegan Action certified), cruelty-free, plastic-free packaged, and uses simple botanical ingredients with full transparency. A small-batch indie brand with no animal ingredients or testing at any stage. Source: meowmeowtweet.com/pages/about

Herbivore Botanicals products are 100% vegan, cruelty-free, and formulated with natural plant and mineral ingredients — no synthetic preservatives, no animal-derived ingredients. All products are manufactured in small batches in the US. Source: herbivorebotanicals.com/pages/about

Badger Balm is Leaping Bunny certified (no animal testing at any stage), USDA Organic certified, and a certified B Corporation. The family-owned company clearly labels all vegan products and donates 10% of after-tax profits to charity. Source: badgerbalm.com/pages/mission-and-values

Cocokind is 100% vegan, PETA-certified cruelty-free, and a certified B Corporation. The company publishes a transparency scorecard on each product showing carbon footprint and supply chain metrics — one of the most detailed disclosure practices in beauty. Source: cocokind.com/pages/our-mission

Ursa Major is Leaping Bunny certified cruelty-free and 100% vegan, with formulas banning over 1,400 questionable ingredients. Made in the US without synthetic fragrances, parabens, sulfates, or animal-derived ingredients. Source: ursamajorvt.com/pages/our-standards

Nature's Path products are USDA Certified Organic with no GMOs, artificial colors, flavors, or preservatives. Family-owned and independently certified organic since 1985. The company also donates 1% of net sales to environmental charities. Source: naturespath.com/en-us/our-story/values/

Bob's Red Mill is 100% employee-owned (ESOP since 2020), uses stone-milling for minimally processed grains, and sells products certified gluten-free, organic, and non-GMO. No artificial additives or preservatives in any product. Source: bobsredmill.com/about-us

Siggi's yogurt is made with 4-6 simple ingredients: milk, live cultures, fruit, and sometimes a small amount of sweetener. No artificial sweeteners, preservatives, flavors, or colors. Among the lowest in added sugar of any flavored yogurt brand. Non-GMO ingredients throughout. Source: siggis.com/pages/about

Once Upon a Farm products are USDA Certified Organic, cold-pressed (not heat-pasteurized) to preserve nutrients, with no added sugar, preservatives, or artificial ingredients. A certified B Corporation. Source: onceuponafarmorganics.com/pages/about

Hu Kitchen chocolate uses organic coconut sugar, no emulsifiers (no soy lecithin), no refined cane sugar, no dairy, and no additives — just cocoa mass, coconut sugar, and simple add-ins. Certified USDA Organic and paleo-friendly throughout. Source: hukitchen.com/pages/hu-facts

Kite Hill makes plant-based dairy alternatives using clean almond milk fermentation with minimal ingredients — no gums, starches, or artificial stabilizers that are common in the category. Non-GMO Project Verified across all products. Source: kite-hill.com/our-story/

Good Culture cottage cheese contains only milk and live active cultures — no gums, starch, preservatives, or artificial ingredients. Uses pasture-raised cow milk and USDA Organic certification. One of the only mass-market cottage cheese brands with a truly clean ingredient list. Source: goodculture.com/pages/about

Purely Elizabeth granola and oatmeal are USDA Organic, Non-GMO Project Verified, and gluten-free certified. Sweetened with coconut sugar (not refined cane sugar), no artificial additives, ancient grains throughout. A certified B Corporation and 1% for the Planet member. Source: purely-elizabeth.com/pages/our-story

Harmless Harvest coconut water is USDA Organic, Fair Trade certified, and processed via high-pressure pasteurization (never heat) to preserve natural nutrients and enzymes. No additives, sweeteners, or 'natural flavors' — just raw coconut water. Certified B Corporation. Source: harmlessharvest.com/pages/our-story

Miyoko's Creamery makes plant-based butter and cheese with clean ingredients — USDA Organic, Non-GMO Project Verified, no carrageenan, no artificial additives. Traditional artisan techniques with minimal, recognizable ingredients. Certified B Corporation. Source: miyokos.com/pages/about

Organic Valley is owned by 1,600+ farmer-members who vote on governance and share all profits. As a cooperative, it cannot be acquired by private equity investors. Farmer-owners have received consistent prices above commodity market rates for 35+ years. Source: organicvalley.coop/about-us/cooperative/

Ocean Spray is owned entirely by ~700 cranberry and grapefruit farmer-members who receive all profits via patronage dividends and elect the board democratically. The cooperative model has protected grower livelihoods since 1930 and prevents PE acquisition. Source: oceanspray.com/en-us/about-ocean-spray

Land O'Lakes is a cooperative owned by ~300,000 farmer-members through 1,700 member co-ops. All profits return to farmer-owners via patronage dividends. Democratic governance means no private equity extraction is possible. Source: landolakesinc.com/about

Tillamook is owned by ~70 farm families in Oregon who share all profits and govern the co-op democratically. With over $1 billion in annual revenue, all returns flow to farmer-owners rather than investors. The cooperative structure has protected local farm families for over 100 years. Source: tillamook.com/our-story/cooperative

Cabot Creamery is owned by ~800 New England farm families through the Agri-Mark cooperative. It is a certified B Corporation (since 2012) and returns all profits to farmer-members via patronage dividends. One of the first cooperatives to achieve B Corp certification. Source: cabotcheese.coop/about-cabot/

USAA is a reciprocal exchange (member-owned cooperative structure) serving US military families. Profits are distributed back to members as dividends and rate reductions rather than to outside shareholders. USAA consistently rates #1 in financial services customer satisfaction. Source: usaa.com/inet/wc/about_usaa_main

Vanguard is owned by its own funds, which are owned by investors — there are no outside shareholders to extract profits. Vanguard passes all economies of scale to investors as lower fees, which is why its expense ratios are among the industry's lowest. Founded explicitly as an anti-extraction structure by John Bogle in 1975. Source: about.vanguard.com/who-we-are/a-remarkable-history/

Ace Hardware is a retailer-owned cooperative — all ~5,700 stores are independently owned and the co-op is governed by its retail members, not outside investors. The cooperative model has protected independent hardware stores from PE acquisition for 100 years. Source: acehardware.com/about-ace

Equal Exchange is 100% worker-owned and cooperatively governed — every employee is an owner with voting rights. It partners directly with farmer cooperatives, paying fair trade premiums above commodity prices. One of the longest-standing worker cooperatives in the US since 1986. Source: equalexchange.coop/about-us

ShopRite is owned by Wakefern Food Corporation, a retailer cooperative owned by ~50 independent grocery families — not outside investors. The cooperative structure has kept ShopRite stores under local, independent ownership since 1946 and cannot be acquired by private equity. Source: wakefern.com/about

Basecamp is fully bootstrapped with no outside investors — the company's founders publish revenue and profit figures voluntarily, operate without a VC-controlled board, and have written extensively about resisting growth-at-all-costs capitalism. Source: 37signals.com/

Buffer publicly lists every employee's salary on its website with the formula used to calculate it, shares company revenue figures publicly, and has practiced radical transparency since 2013. This is the direct opposite of opacity used to enable elite impunity. Source: buffer.com/resources/salary-formula

Kickstarter converted to a Public Benefit Corporation in 2021, legally binding the company to its public mission and preventing shareholder-first governance. The charter prohibits selling user data, commits 5% of after-tax profits to arts/education, and embeds anti-inequality commitments. Source: kickstarter.com/charter

Firefox is developed by the nonprofit Mozilla Foundation, which publishes full financials annually, has no private equity owners, and whose charter explicitly opposes internet domination by a small number of corporations. Mozilla has been a consistent advocate for open internet governance and user privacy. Source: mozilla.org/en-US/about/manifesto/

Wikipedia is operated by the nonprofit Wikimedia Foundation, which publishes full audited financials annually, accepts no advertising, and makes all content freely available. The nonprofit governance model prevents the kind of oligarchic control that enables elite impunity. Source: wikimediafoundation.org/about/

DuckDuckGo collects no personal data, builds no user profiles, and does not share search data. It is profitable without surveillance advertising, demonstrating that tech companies can be transparent and privacy-respecting. Source: duckduckgo.com/privacy

ProtonMail is end-to-end encrypted, operated by a Swiss nonprofit-owned company (Proton Foundation), governed under Swiss privacy law, and cannot be compelled to provide user data to foreign governments. The nonprofit ownership structure prevents acquisition by surveillance-oriented buyers. Source: proton.me/about

Signal is a nonprofit with no advertising, no data collection, and no private equity owners — operated by a 501(c)(3) that cannot be sold or monetized through surveillance. The foundation model makes Signal structurally resistant to the governance patterns that enable elite impunity. Source: signal.org/blog/signal-is-a-nonprofit/

Fairphone publishes its full supplier list, sources conflict-free minerals (verified), and designs phones to be repairable (user-replaceable battery and modules). Its social enterprise charter legally prioritizes people and planet over profit. Source: fairphone.com/en/impact/

Credo Beauty bans over 2,700 ingredients from its Credo Clean Standard, requires full fragrance ingredient disclosure (something major retailers do not demand), and publicly calls out beauty brand greenwashing. Its transparency requirements function as a check on industry impunity. Source: credobeauty.com/pages/the-credo-clean-standard

Leonard Green acquired Joann for $1.6B in 2010, loaded it with $1.1B debt, then drove it to bankruptcy twice in under a year (March 2024, January 2025). Liquidated all 800 stores by May 2025, laying off 19,000 employees with no severance.

Leonard Green owned 2010–2021 and extracted $658M in dividends (Senate Budget Committee report), leaving the 16-hospital safety net system with $2.3B debt and deteriorating facilities. Filed bankruptcy January 2025 with over 1,000 layoffs and 100,000+ creditors including unpaid employees and pensioners.

Audax PE roll-up combining seven regional remodelers (NEWPRO, Minnesota Rusco, Dreamstyle, Alure, others) in 2021 with $150M debt from BlackRock/Apollo/Oaktree. Collapsed October 2025 with Chapter 7 filing listing $100M+ debt vs $50k assets. 1,500+ workers terminated without final paychecks or severance; thousands of homeowners left with unfinished projects and lost deposits.

Taken private in $55B LBO by Saudi PIF consortium in 2025, the largest leveraged buyout in history. Deal loaded EA with $20B in debt (JPMorgan-led financing), shifting from public accountability to sovereign wealth fund control. PIF holds 93.4% stake, with Silver Lake and Affinity Partners holding minority positions.

Acquired by BlackRock-led AI Infrastructure Partnership consortium for $40B in 2025, one of the largest data center acquisitions ever. Deal structured to reach $100B including debt financing, targeting AI infrastructure expansion. Consortium includes Microsoft, Nvidia, MGX, and sovereign wealth funds.

Taken private by Thoma Bravo in $12.3B LBO completed in 2025. Deal loaded the HR software company with $6B in debt (Goldman Sachs-led financing: $5.5B term loan + $500M revolver), making it the largest buyout financing of 2025. Loan rated B-/B3, indicating high default risk. Transaction included minority stake from Abu Dhabi Investment Authority.

Platinum Equity acquired LifeScan from Johnson & Johnson in 2018 for $2.1B, loading it with $1.9B debt. Filed Chapter 11 bankruptcy July 2025 to eliminate 75% of $1.7B debt burden, handing control to creditors. Debt-loading from the LBO made the glucose monitor maker unsustainable despite providing essential medical devices for diabetic patients.

NRDC's Richard Baker acquired Saks as part of his explicit strategy to 'snap up retailers with valuable real estate' and extract property value. Filed Chapter 11 January 2026 with $3B debt after Baker's 2024 Neiman Marcus merger was financed by $2.2B in junk bonds. Baker admitted 'being a retailer, that is too tough' while real estate extraction 'is a place where we can make a lot of money.' Pattern mirrors his bankrupting of Lord & Taylor and Hudson's Bay.

Authentic Brands Group bought Forever 21 from 2020 bankruptcy for $81M and split the company: ABG kept brand IP, licensed it to F21 OpCo LLC which bore operational risk and $1.5B debt. Operating entity filed second bankruptcy March 2025, liquidating 355 stores and laying off 9,200 employees. ABG's extraction model ensures it profits from brand licensing while operators and workers absorb all downside risk. CEO Jamie Salter called it his 'biggest mistake.'

Hyatt Hotels Corporation's executive chairman Thomas Pritzker exchanged over 20 emails with convicted sex offender Jeffrey Epstein between 2010 and 2019, well after Epstein's 2008 conviction. Unsealed DOJ documents show Pritzker making plans to see Epstein and seeking his advice on estate planning matters. Pritzker was named in Virginia Giuffre's deposition as one of several men she was trafficked to, and he resigned from Hyatt's board in February 2026 citing 'terrible judgment' in maintaining contact with Epstein.

Media publication owned by billionaire Mortimer Zuckerman, who maintained regular contact with Jeffrey Epstein from 2008-2015, including dinners at Epstein's home. Unsealed documents show Zuckerman shared sensitive estate planning documents with Epstein in 2008 and considered paying him $21-30 million for financial advice. Most troublingly, Epstein used his relationship with Zuckerman to influence New York Daily News (also Zuckerman-owned) coverage of sex abuse allegations against him, with Zuckerman complying by ordering 'major editing' of stories.

Co-founded by Bill Gates, who met repeatedly with Jeffrey Epstein from 2011-2014 despite Epstein's 2008 conviction for sex crimes involving minors. DOJ documents reveal hundreds of emails, scheduled meetings and meals between them. Gates continued the relationship even after Melinda French Gates raised concerns in 2013. The Gates Foundation is now investigating what it calls a 'small number' of employee meetings with Epstein, who claimed he could mobilize philanthropic resources. Warren Buffett stopped speaking to Gates and may halt donations to the Gates Foundation over the Epstein revelations.
